- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

- **References:** `<Iadtc.12799$L.1063@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <cfaeadf3.0405271151.20c59a4@posting.google.com> <10bfu1iimqi3te9@corp.supernews.com> <6eVtc.34937$Ol3.23233@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <10bi5879obpdo49@corp.supernews.com> <40b94629.105551185@news.optonline.net>`

```

On 29-May-2004, robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> Unnecessary REDEFINES are the signature feature of Bad Cobol. It drives me
> crazy. Why don't they write:

REDEFINES work when it fits the data.   When you are reading data that might
have different formats, redefines work better than anything else.


>  01  textString value "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog's back.".
>       12  testStringElt occurs 80 indexed testStrongldx pic x.

That didn't use to be possible.

> Four lines vs. eleven.

What's the big deal about counting lines?

> On the one hand they use abbreviations to avoid 'unnecessary typing'; on the
> other hand they pad the program with superfluous words and spacing.

Who's "they"?    I don't mind typing.

P.S.  Why did you make your text string so long?   No need to have the word
"back".   Jump over the lazy dog.
```

## Thread: Programs expecting "initial values" — 56 this month / 57 total

_Thread started [2004-05](2004-05.md#t-070b29c69b)._

#### ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-01T02:14:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>IMHO ...
>
>Any program that is expecting (requires) initial values (rather than "last used
>state") for all Working-Storage items should have the "IS INITIAL" phrase in
the
>Program-ID paragraph.  (Those still using '74 Standard compilers need not reply
><G>)
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>using other approaches, but "in general" it would seem that above should
provide
>"safe" programming techniques for programs that may or may NOT be called by
>"unsafe" calling (activating) programs.

Conversely, every reentrant program should say IS RECURSIVE, because the
Standard says:

"The program cannot be activated while it is active unless RECURSIVE is
specified."

Examples of reentrant programs are DB2 Stored Procedures and plain-vanilla CICS
programs, which load but one copy of the Procedure Division and permit parallel
execution of multiple transactions and queries. For years, IBM has been giving
each invocation its own initialized Working-Storage, which, in effect, turns it
into Local-Storage. It does the same for programs CALLed by those main programs.
Neither are required to use the words RECURSIVE, INITIAL nor Local-Storage.
Second-level called programs might choose to use INITIAL to be re-initialized
for each call within a 'thread', but the first one is free.

When IBM provides a 2002-compliant compiler, will every CICS program need to be
changed? 

Micro Focus supports reentrant (non-recursive) programs via two compiler
options, one of which works just like CICS and DB2. When they get to 2002
compliance, must those programs be changed to RECURSIVE, even though they are
not?

The answer to both rhetorical questions is no. The deviations will be 'vendor
extensions'. I cannot think of any extensions or changes required in ordinary
'85 Cobol programs (unless they use pointers). Many/most are already
2002-compliant.

IBM mainframers looking at a large percentage of their Cobol programs
non-compliant might throw up their hands and lose interest in the 2002 Standard.
All because of the 'high cost' of adding the words RECURSIVE and INITIAL to
every CICS program and DB2 stored procedure. 

The problem could have been avoided with a definition of 'run unit' that makes
it clear a run unit begins with a  transaction or query rather than the loading
of a program. In other words, one that's thread-oriented rather than
process-oriented.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2004-06-01T02:20:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6KRuc.16622$_k3.399803@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net>`

```


Robert Wagner wrote:
> (snip) 
> Examples of reentrant programs are DB2 Stored Procedures and plain-vanilla CICS
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> changed? 

I have seen a lot of IBM mainframe COBOL CICS programs with "IS 
INITIAL" added to the PROGRAM-ID, as we upgraded to LE-enabled COBOL 
compilers.  It seems to be most helpful for pseudo-conversational 
programs with BMS maps.  Not all of our CICS COBOL programs are 
pseudo-conversational.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T07:13:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<M6fvc.113335$hH.1997156@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<40bd1269.148480454@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <6KRuc.16622$_k3.399803@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <40bd1269.148480454@news.optonline.net>`

```


Robert Wagner wrote:
> (snip) 
> 
> What happens differently when IS INITIAL is added? What problem does it solve?

It seems to prevent garbage in BMS maps.  Prior to COBOL II, old COBOL 
always initialized unvalued working storarge to low-values.  Starting 
with COBOL II, you could get random data in working-storage unless a 
specific COBOL II runtime library option or LE/MVS option was set to 
clear uninitialized storage.  INITIAL might not really help that, but 
I see it used in a lot of newer CICS programs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Andreas Lerch <andreas@andreas-lerch.de>
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T16:07:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20040602.16073663@rechner12.lerch.home>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <6KRuc.16622$_k3.399803@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <40bd1269.148480454@news.optonline.net> <M6fvc.113335$hH.1997156@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```


>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>> Ursprüngliche Nachricht <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<

Am 02.06.04, 07:13:48, schrieb Arnold Trembley 
<arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> zum Thema Re: Programs expecting 
"initial values":


> Robert Wagner wrote:
> > (snip)
> >
> > What happens differently when IS INITIAL is added? What problem does 
it solve?

> It seems to prevent garbage in BMS maps.  Prior to COBOL II, old COBOL
> always initialized unvalued working storarge to low-values.  Starting
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I see it used in a lot of newer CICS programs.

Hello all

For COBOL-II there is a CICS Runtime-Option: WSCLAER
For Cobol for MVS there is a Runtime-Option equivalen to WSCLAER 
inside the CICS option Module: CEECOPT (i think this is the name but 
read the migration guide)

both of them set the content of WS (dynamic storage) to low-values and 
the value-assigned-fields are moved in the initial code. Therefor try:
CBL OPT LIST NOOFSET
on the first cobol line

we moved from Cobol-VS to Cobol-II by code-conversion and a big 
project
we moved from COBOL-II to COBOL-MVS without code-conversion, only by 
changing the Installation
both Cobol-II and Cobol-MVS programs can be used concurently within 
one runtime and without absolutely no sourcechange and problems.

Einen schoenen Tag

Andreas Lerch
```

> Robert Wagner wrote:

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

I can certainly remember that old COBOL, 1970s versions, did not
automatically initialise  working storage data items without
associated VALUE clauses, COBOL VS2 may well have, though I can't
remember that specifically.  It would always be a good idea to check
the compiler documentation first.  Arguably unless the standard says
that working storage is always initialised, which it doesn't, then it
is safer and more portable to assume that it isn't so initialised.

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T14:42:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0406021342.73dffa4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bd1269.148480454@news.optonline.net> <M6fvc.113335$hH.1997156@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <6dd8322.0406020930.8099ea9@posting.google.com> <c9l4qj$c81$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote 

> Does anyone else remember having a program do... whacky stuff, not 
> reproducible from one compile to the next, which it stopped doing when an 
> VALUE clause was added?

Yes. Back in the late 60s when core memory was used.  When two
particular programs were run one after the other the second would
complete as if the data file (tape) was empty.  A later rerun of the
second would work correctly.  If turned out that the 'EoF' flags in
the two coincided in memory and the second program did not initialise
this, thus using the specific value set by the first as indicating the
end was at hand.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-01T03:24:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UFSuc.18758$be.2374@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net>`

```
Huh ???
   RECURSIVE and REENTRANT have little or nothing to do with each other.

RECURSIVE is appropriate when there are multiple copies of the same program
active IN THE SAME RUN-UNIT.

REENTRANT (a term that is *never* used or implied in the Standard - neither
supported nor probhitied) applies to environments (Operating systems,
subsystems, whatever) which allow multiple occurrences of the same program(s)
simultaneously in different run-units (tasks, or whatever the system calls
them).

If multiple simultaneous run-units are involved, then reentrant may be an issue;
if a single run-unit (with multiple simultaneous occurences of the same program)
is being talked about then recursive is what is of interest.

The quote you used below assumes that you understand what "activated" means.
See the ISO 2002 Standard, page 385 (and following) which starts with

"14.5.2.1 State of a function, method, or program
The state of a function, method, or program at any point in time in a run unit
may be active or inactive. When a function, method, or program is activated, its
state may also be initial or last-used."

CICS *reentrant* programs are NOT in the same run-unit.  If the same program
were to be activated (while already ative) a 2nd (to Nth) time in a single CICS
run-unit, then it WOULD require the RECURSIVE attribute.

In the IBM (mainframe) environment, for example, all compilers from VS COBOL II
onwards supported "true" re-entrant code - while recursive code was not
introduced untile Entprirse COBOL.  Furthermore, I beleive (but haven't verified
it - and am not certain WHY you want to do this), you can compile a program with
the NORENT compiler option (creating non-reentrant code) and still use the
RECURSIVE attribute.

Generally, (and specifically in IBM environments) these two concepts are QUITE
distinct.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T01:22:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40bcfb90.142630754@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <UFSuc.18758$be.2374@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>Huh ???
>   RECURSIVE and REENTRANT have little or nothing to do with each other.
>
>RECURSIVE is appropriate when there are multiple copies of the same program
>active IN THE SAME RUN-UNIT.

The Standard defines Run Unit as a compiled program, whether running or not:

3.3 A conforming run unit
A conforming run unit is one that:
1) is composed of one or more runtime modules, each resulting from a successful
compilation of a conforming compilation unit, and
2) complies with the explicitly stated provisions and specifications of standard
COBOL with respect to the runtime behavior of that run unit.

The definition at issue here is an Activation:

14: Procedure division 
14.7.1.1.1 Active state
A function, method, or program may be activated recursively. Therefore, several
instances of a function, method,
or program may be active at once. When a rule indicates that the active state of
a runtime element is tested,
-> it is in the active state if any instance of the element is active. <-
[emphasis added]

An instance of a function is placed in an active state when it is successfully
activated and remains active until the
execution of a STOP statement or an implicit or explicit function format of the
EXIT statement within this instance
of this function.

State of a function, method, object, or program
An instance of a method is placed in an active state when it is successfully
activated and remains active until the
execution of a STOP statement or implicit or explicit method format of the EXIT
statement within this instance of
this method.

An instance of a program is placed in an active state when it is successfully
activated by the operating system or
->successfully called from a runtime element.<- 
[emphasis added. CICS is such a runtime element.]
 An instance of a program remains active until the execution of one of
the following:
� a STOP statement
� in a called program, an implicit or explicit program format of an EXIT
statement within that program
� a GOBACK statement within either that same called program or a program that is
not under the control of a calling runtime element.
--- end quotation ---

>REENTRANT (a term that is *never* used or implied in the Standard - neither
>supported nor probhitied) applies to environments (Operating systems,
>subsystems, whatever) which allow multiple occurrences of the same program(s)
>simultaneously in different run-units (tasks, or whatever the system calls
>them).

The wording of the Standard is "active run unit". It doesn't mention tasks,
threads, transactions, etc. 

>If multiple simultaneous run-units are involved, then reentrant may be an
>issue; if a single run-unit (with multiple simultaneous occurences of the same
>program) is being talked about then recursive is what is of interest.

The basic problem is that the Standard DOESN'T acknowledge the possibility of
reentrancy. It's language implies they only way for two instances of a run unit
to be active is recursion. Based on that premise, it says a run unit is active
if any instance is active. Interpreted literally, two CICS transactions running
in parallel and ending with GOBACK are two instances of a single active run
unit. 

>The quote you used below assumes that you understand what "activated" means.
>See the ISO 2002 Standard, page 385 (and following) which starts with
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>CICS run-unit, then it WOULD require the RECURSIVE attribute.

A "CICS run-unit" makes little sense because mainframe CICS wasn't written in
Cobol. In the language of the Standard, CICS is a "runtime element".

If CICS was written in Cobol, as in fact it was in a Micro Focus product, you
agree with me -- the Cobol programs it calls must be compiled with RECURSIVE. 

Both versions of CICS call programs the same way. I don't understand why
application programs need be written differently.

> Furthermore, I beleive (but haven't verified
>it - and am not certain WHY you want to do this), you can compile a program
>with the NORENT compiler option (creating non-reentrant code) and still use the
>RECURSIVE attribute.

Reentrant programs require a stack per thread. Recursive programs usually have
only one thread, therefore need only one stack. One would use that combination
in a 'true' recursive program to avoid wasting time setting up a stack per
instance.

>Generally, (and specifically in IBM environments) these two concepts are QUITE
>distinct.

They are in all operating systems that support both. I was talking about the
'Cobol Standard environment', which does not support reentrancy and uses
language intended for recursion that accidentally applies to both.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T02:33:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<E%avc.18563$Tn6.15607@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <UFSuc.18758$be.2374@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bcfb90.142630754@news.optonline.net>`

```
You are correct that the 2002 Standard defines what a "run unit" is - which is
(slightly) different from what a CONFOMRING run unit is.  See page 15,

"4.166 run unit: One or more runtime modules that interact with one another and
that function, at execution time, as an entity to provide problem solutions."

As far as "reentrancy" goes, make certain that you read what the SCOPE is of the
2002 Standard.  On page 1 it states (among other things),

"This International Standard does not specify:
   ...
- The sharing of resources other than files among run units."

***

For a discussion (and diagram) of the WELL defined definition of what is and is
not a run unit under CICS, see:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/dfhp3a00/1.4.5.2
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T03:18:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40bd417e.160552894@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <UFSuc.18758$be.2374@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bcfb90.142630754@news.optonline.net> <E%avc.18563$Tn6.15607@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>For a discussion (and diagram) of the WELL defined definition of what is and is
>not a run unit under CICS, see:
>
> http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/dfhp3a00/1.4.5.2

It says "A main, or level 1 program can use the COBOL GOBACK or STOP RUN
commands, or the CICS RETURN command to terminate and return to CICS"

The 2002 Cobol Standard makes a distinction between GOBACK and STOP RUN.
The latter terminates a 'run unit' whereas the former does not.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T11:33:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0Wivc.20429$be.11828@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <UFSuc.18758$be.2374@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bcfb90.142630754@news.optonline.net> <E%avc.18563$Tn6.15607@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bd417e.160552894@news.optonline.net>`

```
HUH???

A GOBACK in a MAIN (level 1 in CICS terminology) in the ISO 2002 Standard is the
same as a Stop Run. See GR2 which says,

"2) If a GOBACK statement is executed in a program that is not under the control
of a calling runtime element, the program operates as if executing a STOP
statement without any optional phrases."

This is EXACTLY the same as what CICS does.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T02:15:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40be7099.238159620@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <UFSuc.18758$be.2374@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bcfb90.142630754@news.optonline.net> <E%avc.18563$Tn6.15607@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bd417e.160552894@news.optonline.net> <0Wivc.20429$be.11828@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>HUH???
>
>A GOBACK in a MAIN (level 1 in CICS terminology) in the ISO 2002 Standard is
the
>same as a Stop Run. See GR2 which says,
>
>"2) If a GOBACK statement is executed in a program that is not under the
control
>of a calling runtime element, the program operates as if executing a STOP
>statement without any optional phrases."
>
>This is EXACTLY the same as what CICS does.

No, CICS is a "calling runtime element". In the Good Old Days, STOP RUN would
terminate the CICS task, usually bringing the wrath of a systems programmer. We
always exited with GOBACK. 

I assume they've fixed STOP RUN by now, making it do an orderly return.

 
>Bill Klein
> wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>> The latter terminates a 'run unit' whereas the former does not.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T08:53:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c9kt4v$1ert$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <UFSuc.18758$be.2374@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bcfb90.142630754@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert.deletethis@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:40bcfb90.142630754@news.optonline.net...

> The basic problem is that the Standard DOESN'T acknowledge the possibility
of
> reentrancy.

No, I don't think that's right.  Let's start with the definition of "run
unit" on Page 385:  "A run unit is an independent entity that may be
executed without communicating with, or being coordinated with, any other
run unit except that it may process files and messages or set and test
switches that were written or will be read by other run units."

Further useful terms and clarifications in the immediate vicinity:  "A
runtime module results from compiling a compilation unit.  Each runtime
module contains one or more runtime elements.  A runtime element results
from the compilation of a function, method or program.  ... A run unit and
each of its contained runtime modules may also contain resources and data
storage areas needed for the execution and intercommunication of the runtime
elements contained in the run unit."

Thus, if two or more instances of a group of runtime modules interact in
*any* way, they form part of the same *run unit* because they are *not
independent*.  .

> It's language implies they only way for two instances of a run unit
> to be active is recursion.

No, I don't think that's right.  Same page, "14.5.21.1, Active state"
defines "active state" as meaningful only for a *function, method, or
program*, not for a *run unit*.   There is no definition in the standard for
"active state" as it applies to "run unit", only to runtime elements within
runtime modules used in a given run unit.  Moreover, the standard has
nothing to say about the *interaction* of two run units precisely because
each run unit is *independent* of any other run unit or runtime module
within such a separate run unit.

In the case of programs compiled for Unisys MCP systems as an example, all
executions of a program share a *single* copy of the set of object code
segments in memory, and the manner and sequence in which those individual
object code segments are loaded into memory is dependent not on an
*individual* execution but the aggregate effect of *all* the simultaneous
executions.  Each instance has its own memory space for *data*, but the code
space is shared.   The standard doesn't care, because the data is handled
separately -- as required for independence.

> Based on that premise, it says a run unit is active
> if any instance is active.

No, I don't think that's right.  See above, and perhaps to clarify:  If any
sort of interaction occurs -- including, for example, the sharing of data
spaces -- between two run units, aside from the "files, messages and
switches" allowed by the standard, then these two "run units" are actually
part of a single run unit.   The standard has no need to define reentrancy;
it has no meaning so long as "run units" operate independently.

> Interpreted literally, two CICS transactions running
> in parallel and ending with GOBACK are two instances of a single active
run
> unit.

I don't speak CICS, but again, I think you're misusing terms here.  "Run
units" are not "active" in COBOL standard terms, only functions, methods,
and programs can be "active".   If CICS expects the two instantiations of a
runtime module to be totally independent, then those instantiations form two
separate run units.  If it doesn't, then they don't.   Last paragraph of
"14.5.1 Run unit organization":  A run unit may additionally contain runtime
modules and data storage areas derived from the compilation of compilation
units written in languages other than COBOL; in this case the requirements
for the relationship and interaction between the COBOL and the non-COBOL
modules are defined by the implementor."

> They are in all operating systems that support both. I was talking about
the
> 'Cobol Standard environment', which does not support reentrancy and uses
> language intended for recursion that accidentally applies to both.

Again, that's not the way I see it.  The standard has no reason to describe
whether multiple run units -- or multipe runtime modules operating
independently as run units -- can be reentrant or not precisely because, if
they aren't independent they're part of the same run unit to begin with, and
if they are independent there's nothing for the standard to say about it
because they do not interact.

The standard does not define the *interaction* between elements which it
describes as *wholly independent* from one another.   Is that your basic
objection?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T02:15:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40be7258.238606952@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <UFSuc.18758$be.2374@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bcfb90.142630754@news.optonline.net> <c9kt4v$1ert$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert.deletethis@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>independent*.  .

OK .. a multithreading reentrant runtime is a single "run unit", as is a
recursive runtime.

>> It's language implies they only way for two instances of a run unit
>> to be active is recursion.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>within such a separate run unit.

No change .. the runtime is still one run unit. But it must be called RECURSIVE
in PROGRAM-ID.

>In the case of programs compiled for Unisys MCP systems as an example, all
>executions of a program share a *single* copy of the set of object code
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>separately -- as required for independence.

That's not an example of multithreading nor recursion. As you say, that's
multiple run units, commonly called multi-tasking.

>> Based on that premise, it says a run unit is active
>> if any instance is active.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>it has no meaning so long as "run units" operate independently.

If they share storage, the standard defines them as a single RECURSIVE run unit.

>> Interpreted literally, two CICS transactions running
>> in parallel and ending with GOBACK are two instances of a single active
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>modules are defined by the implementor."

CICS provides synchronous and asynchronous facilities for communicating between
active programs. The quotation seems to say that IBM, not the standard, has
classified what would normally be multiple run units as one run unit.

If CICS were written in Cobol, would that change anything? I think not because
it would still be using non-Cobol memory management facilities provided by CICS.
When the standard says "contain runtime modules and data storage areas derived
from the compilation of compilation units written in languages other than
COBOL", I interpret that to mean 'use services not provided by COBOL'. It
doesn't matter whether the services are written in Cobol or another language.

The addition of pointers in 2002 enables a 100% Cobol run unit using pipelines
and other queues, shared memory, etc. 

>> They are in all operating systems that support both. I was talking about
>>the
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>objection?

No. My objections are that the Cobol standard does not acknowledge reentrancy
and, with imprecise language, classifies reentrant programs as recursive. 

I suggest replacing the concept of "run unit" with the more descriptive 'thread
of execution'. A recursive program, like a 'normal' program, has but one thread.
It requires only a Local-Storage per instance. A multithreaded program
additionally requires a call stack per instance and a way to lock
Working-Storage (or individual variables therein).  If this were in the
standard, the locking would be *automatic* rather than relying on programmer
skill in calling outside services. The result would be more reliable
multithreading Cobol programs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T14:06:43-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c9o3t3$j46$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <UFSuc.18758$be.2374@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bcfb90.142630754@news.optonline.net> <c9kt4v$1ert$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <40be7258.238606952@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert.deletethis@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:40be7258.238606952@news.optonline.net...

> No. My objections are that the Cobol standard does not acknowledge
reentrancy
> and, with imprecise language, classifies reentrant programs as recursive.

As far as I can tell, "reentrancy" is wholly outside the purview of the
standard.  Multiple instances of a program either interact or they do not.

> I suggest replacing the concept of "run unit" with the more descriptive
'thread
> of execution'.

OK ... can you ensure that doing so won't cause confusion elsewhere in the
standard?

> A recursive program, like a 'normal' program, has but one thread.
> It requires only a Local-Storage per instance.

Technically, the entire DATA DIVISION is optional.  A recursive program may
not require even that much (though I agree that its capabilities would be
limited!).

> A multithreaded program
> additionally requires a call stack per instance and a way to lock
> Working-Storage (or individual variables therein).

I'm not yet convinced that "multithreaded" is some inherent characteristic
of the *program* so much as it is how the program is used and in what ways
it prevents itself from being misused.

> If this were in the
> standard, the locking would be *automatic* rather than relying on
programmer
> skill in calling outside services.

I am also not yet convinced that providing such control mechanisms directly
in the syntax of the language is such a terrible idea (see below).  The
problem is doing so in a way that will not require implementors to provide
mechanisms that are inappropriate for the target environment.

> The result would be more reliable
> multithreading Cobol programs.

The way Unisys MCP COBOLs have prevented recursion before it was allowed was
that the compiler, behind the user's back, generated code to ensure that
they weren't invoked recursively, precisely because the COBOL standards
(starting in '74) required that it be that way.  There is no inherent
problem with recursion in the MCP environment -- users are expected to take
whatever steps are necessary to protect their data.  In the case of COBOL74
and COBOL85, the requirements of the standard preventing recursion do so on
behalf of the user, though other extensions are provided to allow the user
to circumvent these safeguards.   It is not always possible to determine
whether a program *is* recursive or not; sometimes the only way to find out
is at execution time.  The mechanisms and structures that are used by this
behind-the-back code are actually available to the user of COBOL as a Unisys
extension, and to other languages as well (see below), and may be
selectively or periodically applied by the programmer at execution time.

All executions of a given object code file share a single instantiation of
it in the system memory, which as I see it means all user object code in the
MCP environment is by nature reentrant.

This whole topic which I would call "selective synchronicity" also appears
to be closely related to something that's been in "Candidate features for a
future revision" of the COBOL85 standard for a while (since early 1998, for
sure):  "asynchronous processing".  This is one of the three features that
WG4 has asked we *consider* for the next standard if we get all the
mandatory work done.  The members of the committee are trying very hard to
get that mandatory stuff into a solid Committee Draft for ISO review by
December, and I don't think the committee member to whom this is assigned
has had a lot of time or other resources to address it.  A formal paper
describing the mechanisms you propose as well as the specific changes that
would be necessary to support them would, I'm sure, be welcomed, though
there's a good chance it still couldn't be polished up in time to make it
into the planned 2008 standard.

I'm not yet convinced that the features that have been available to users of
Unisys MCP COBOL (and of course ALGOL and NEWP) for some decades aren't an
appropriate model here.  These features are extensions to COBOL to deal with
"semi-synchronization" of otherwise-asyncrhonous independent tasks.  They
include the ability to declare USAGE LOCK, EVENT and TASK items; to LOCK and
RELEASE lock items; to CAUSE events and WAIT until their status is HAPPENED;
and to CALL and CONTINUE synchronous-but-otherwise-independent tasks and to
PROCESS independent ones, in both cases being able to observe the fate of
such tasks, using locks and events as parameters.    The proper use of such
features to protect the user's data remains the programmer's responsibility,
just as it is when the authors of system software make use of them in ALGOL
or NEWP.     It is not up to the standards committee to *prevent* the user
from shooting himself in the foot, and I'm not sure how to determine, for
two arbitrary programs, when it is appropriate for them to be sharing
information and when it is not, particularly if this determination is to be
made at compile time.

I for one would be very interested in seeing a paper providing whatever
detail changes to the 2002 standard you would suggest in this area, and
would also suspect the person targeted with the responsibility for
developing a proposal for "Asynchronous Processing" as a possible feature
for the 2008 standard would welcome any suggestions.

       -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-06T13:44:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40c31258.173929693@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <UFSuc.18758$be.2374@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bcfb90.142630754@news.optonline.net> <c9kt4v$1ert$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <40be7258.238606952@news.optonline.net> <c9o3t3$j46$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert.deletethis@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>standard.  Multiple instances of a program either interact or they do not.

>I'm not yet convinced that "multithreaded" is some inherent characteristic
>of the *program* so much as it is how the program is used and in what ways
>it prevents itself from being misused.

You seem to be describing 'CICS-style' transaction processors, which don't
communicate with each other. I'm talking about 'true' multithreaded programs,
which explicitly launch threads and communicate between them. 

>I am also not yet convinced that providing such control mechanisms directly
>in the syntax of the language is such a terrible idea (see below).  The
>problem is doing so in a way that will not require implementors to provide
>mechanisms that are inappropriate for the target environment.

If the operating system doesn't support multithreading, the compiler shouldn't
be expected to provide it.

>The way Unisys MCP COBOLs have prevented recursion before it was allowed was
>that the compiler, behind the user's back, generated code to ensure that
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>selectively or periodically applied by the programmer at execution time.

Compilers I've worked on check for recursion on entry to a program, but not on
entry to a paragraph. I've written recursive code at the paragraph level by
maintaining 'the stack' (Local-Storage) in a table. 

>All executions of a given object code file share a single instantiation of
>it in the system memory, which as I see it means all user object code in the
>MCP environment is by nature reentrant.

Most modern operating systems do the same -- one copy of procedural code,
distinct data segments per instance. That's reentrancy but it's not 'true'
multithreading.

>I for one would be very interested in seeing a paper providing whatever
>detail changes to the 2002 standard you would suggest in this area, and
>would also suspect the person targeted with the responsibility for
>developing a proposal for "Asynchronous Processing" as a possible feature
>for the 2008 standard would welcome any suggestions.

Micro Focus must have such a paper, internally. They extended Cobol with START
and WAIT syntax to provide multithreading, and also supply a function library.
In my opinion, their approach falls short in two ways:
1. It lacks automatic locking of shared memory (aka Critical Section code)
2. It is closely tied to the Unix/MS multithreading model. 

In order for multithreading to be approved by J4, I think it would have to be
more generalized than the Unix model for two reasons -- avoiding turf battles
and being compatible with better technologies that will develop in the future.

Thank you for the implied request but I'm not the right person to write such a
model. An academician would be better equipped.

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-06T17:41:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5HIwc.23598$Tn6.4036@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert.deletethis@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:40c31258.173929693@news.optonline.net...
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
<snip>
> >I am also not yet convinced that providing such control mechanisms directly
> >in the syntax of the language is such a terrible idea (see below).  The
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This is why the 2002 Standard has the "processor dependent" category.  It
defines standard semantics/syntax for features that are appropriate for some -
but not all processors.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-07T10:34:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ca28vc$1del$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <UFSuc.18758$be.2374@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bcfb90.142630754@news.optonline.net> <c9kt4v$1ert$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <40be7258.238606952@news.optonline.net> <c9o3t3$j46$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <40c31258.173929693@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert.deletethis@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:40c31258.173929693@news.optonline.net...

> You seem to be describing 'CICS-style' transaction processors, which don't
> communicate with each other. I'm talking about 'true' multithreaded
programs,
> which explicitly launch threads and communicate between them.

What I'm actually saying is that I'm not convinced the standard needs to be
concerned about the difference, or about automatically accounting for it.

> If the operating system doesn't support multithreading, the compiler
shouldn't
> be expected to provide it.

As Bill has pointed out, that's the point of the Processor-Dependent
specification.  What I would prefer is to see a specification for which this
specification wouldn't be needed.


> Compilers I've worked on check for recursion on entry to a program, but
not on
> entry to a paragraph. I've written recursive code at the paragraph level
by
> maintaining 'the stack' (Local-Storage) in a table.

How, in *standard* COBOL, do you enter a program anywhere but at its
beginning?  (This begs, for example, the question of declaring and Unisys
MCP multiple-entry-point libraries using the Explicit Library Declaration
extensions, which do their "housekeeping stuff" before making the entry
points available to other programs)?

> Most modern operating systems do the same -- one copy of procedural code,
> distinct data segments per instance. That's reentrancy but it's not 'true'
> multithreading.

Multithreading and reentrancy are two different things; it seems to me to
have been the fact that the COBOL85 standard does not address *reentrancy*
that precipitated this discussion in the first place!
> Micro Focus must have such a paper, internally. They extended Cobol with
START
> and WAIT syntax to provide multithreading, and also supply a function
library.
> In my opinion, their approach falls short in two ways:
> 1. It lacks automatic locking of shared memory (aka Critical Section code)
> 2. It is closely tied to the Unix/MS multithreading model.

Micro Focus may have such a paper internally, but it is not in a form
suitable for incorporation into the ANSI standard.   The extensions Unisys
provides for the COBOL85 MCP implementation are  documented in the COBOL85
reference materials -- the EVENT, LOCK and TASK datatypes, the
PROGRAM-LIBRARY section of the data division, and the CAUSE, PROCESS and
WAIT statements along with extensions to SET and CALL; I happen to think
this forms a pretty slick approach to control of asynchronicity applicable
generally, but I don't have time or energy right now to develop the proposal
for the committee (and besides it's already been assigned to someone else).

I agree.  I think some features of asynchronicity are common to all -- in
particular, a data type that can be set or reset to prevent an asynchronous
task from accessing an item (USAGE LOCK in Unisys MCP terms), and a
semaphore to indicate that an asynchronous task may proceed (USAGE EVENT and
the CAUSE and WAIT statements).  I haven't seen the need *yet* to export
paragraph-names as such as distinct from the possibility of exporting
program-id's.

 > In order for multithreading to be approved by J4, I think it would have
to be
> more generalized than the Unix model for two reasons -- avoiding turf
battles
> and being compatible with better technologies that will develop in the
future.

On the other hand, taking the best of existing implementations, and/or
combining features from several implementations, that provide a certain
functionality is not such a bad idea.  Certainly OO COBOL evolved between CD
1.4 which was the basis of several implementations and its ultimate
expression in ISO/IEC 1989:2002, and I strongly suspect many of the stimuli
for those differences can be found in field experience with those early
implementations.

I agree that it would need to be as general as possible.   The suggestion
that J4 must anticipate the details of better technologies that will develop
after the specification is written strikes me as rather an onerous
requirement, however!  We do what we can; better to be as general as
possible so that implementors are free to make use of environment features
as they are developed!

> Thank you for the implied request but I'm not the right person to write
such a
> model. An academician would be better equipped.

I wouldn't describe any of the people currently on J4 as "academicians".
Sometimes, however, it becomes necessary to frame a topic in terms that you
don't choose -- "asynchronous processing", in this case, is a project with
some impetus from WG4 and thus a proposal couched in those terms has a
chance of being examined for the 2008 standard; "multithreading" is not on
the list of features WG4 is willing to allow into the 2008 standard, and
thus a proposal for the "multithreading" feature would be much more
difficult to sell!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert.deletethis@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:40c31258.173929693@news.optonline.net...

>> Compilers I've worked on check for recursion on entry to a program, but
>not on
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>beginning? 

procedure division.
    perform recursive-paragraph.


recursive-paragraph.
    add 1 to stack-pointer
    if  ....
       perform recursive-paragraph
    end-if
    subtract 1 from stack-pointer.

If the machine does not have a call stack, make the outside perform ..thru
-exit.

>Multithreading and reentrancy are two different things; it seems to me to
>have been the fact that the COBOL85 standard does not address *reentrancy*
>that precipitated this discussion in the first place!

It was the absence of multithreading (asynchronous processing) in the 2002
standard.

> The extensions Unisys
>provides for the COBOL85 MCP implementation are  documented in the COBOL85
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>the CAUSE and WAIT statements). 

Given that MCP and Unix are so similar, it would be easy to vett one against the
other to find acceptable terminology. For example, Unix's MUTEX would be changed
to an English word like LOCK or BUSY.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-06-01T12:06:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0406011106.2e64ce66@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> Examples of reentrant programs are DB2 Stored Procedures and plain-vanilla CICS
> programs, which load but one copy of the Procedure Division and permit parallel
> execution of multiple transactions and queries. For years, IBM has been giving
> each invocation its own initialized Working-Storage, which, in effect, turns it
> into Local-Storage. It does the same for programs CALLed by those main programs.

What you descibe as an 'example of reentrant' is not reentrant at all.
 It is just a shared code segment between separate tasks.  If each
task has its own W-S and stack and the code segment is read only (ie
the code isn't modified) then it is just like any other system that
shares read-only segments between tasks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-01T19:38:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kX4vc.17946$Tn6.11443@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406011106.2e64ce66@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard,
   Maybe it's because I come from an IBM mainframe background, but what you
describe as "not reentrant" and "just like any other system that shares
read-only segments between tasks" is EXACTLY what I thought reentrant meant.
(See my other post in this thread).

What is the definition of reentrant that you use?

For what I am "used to" see (for example)

  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/asmp1003/8.5.1

which says,

"The RENT option  indicates to the linker  that the object module  is
reenterable and can  be used by more than one task at a time.  This type of
module cannot be modified by itself or any other module when it is  running.
The assembler  RENT option can be used  to assist in determining whether the
object module is  reentrant.  NORENT indicates that the  object module is not
reentrant."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-06-01T19:44:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0406011844.2feaba8b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406011106.2e64ce66@posting.google.com> <kX4vc.17946$Tn6.11443@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote

>    Maybe it's because I come from an IBM mainframe background, but what you
> describe as "not reentrant" and "just like any other system that shares
> read-only segments between tasks" is EXACTLY what I thought reentrant meant.
> (See my other post in this thread).

Back in the 60s code was typically 'self-modifying' in that the code
segment would be directly changed during program execution, for
example by an ALTER or by a PERFORM changing code at the EXIT to cause
a return.

ie the code segment was not 'read only', this segment could not be
shared or re-entered in any way.

By having a separate PERFORM stack and redirected ALTERs the code
could be generated so it was not written to and thus could be shared
by separate tasks. This has been standard in modern machines for
decades and even the lowly 80286 could protect code segments from
being written to.

> "The RENT option  indicates to the linker  that the object module  is
> reenterable and can  be used by more than one task at a time.  This type of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> reentrant."

Exactly.  It can be used by more than one task, ie by more than one
run-unit, at the same time.  This is only useful at the operating
system level and merely saves memory space and reload time.  ie it is
a re-entrant _code_segment_ merely by virtue of not writing to itself,
not a re-entrant _program_ or _library_.

MicroFocus Level II Cobol runtime on Concurrent-CP/M-86 used that 20
years ago so that we could run 4 or 5 users on a 1 megabyte machine
(8086).

Within a single run-unit a program is reentrant if the _same_ task can
enter it when it is still active.  For example, within one run-unit,
program A calls B which calls A-dash which is an ENTRY within program
A.  Or similar with messagepeek and callbacks.

This re-entrancy within the run-unit and requires Local Storage or
equivalent, but would share the one Working-Storage.  It _also_
requires that the conditions of non-modified code be met and that
there be a separate PERFORM stack and ALTER table (or no ALTER).

Recursive is self-reentrant in that it directly or indirectly
re-enters itself (whereas A -> B -> A-dash or even A -> A-dash is
re-entrant but not recursive).

In the case of DLLs or shared objects (.so) these may be activated by
different run-units.  A simple DLL may only have one data area and
this would be used regardless of which run-unit calls it.  This maybe
where RW got his 'idea' that another program could get the data from
another program.  In some cases this is actually the required
behaviour, though this is usually serial reusability and not
reentrancy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T13:47:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eUkvc.18334$eH1.8236490@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406011106.2e64ce66@posting.google.com> <kX4vc.17946$Tn6.11443@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <217e491a.0406011844.2feaba8b@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0406011844.2feaba8b@posting.google.com...
>
> By having a separate PERFORM stack and redirected ALTERs the code
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> being written to.

"Lowly" 80286?

The '286 was a tremendous technological achievement and a Great Leap Forward
evan Mao would have been proud of.

In its time the '286 was the cat's pajamas.

Then again, weren't we all.
```

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <eUkvc.18334$eH1.8236490@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com>,

> DD
As an insight to all this discussion, COBOL 60, was in part a major 
description of the Univac I. I believe, but can't swear, that all of the
short range committee members had U 1 experience in common.

That being said, again, the growth of hardware is maped to todays
definitions. On the U 1, a run to run program (it's only operating
system) routinely cleared all memory to zeros prior to loading the
next program. Seems this simplicity is gone because of hardware
developments to speed up execution time. And it also seems that
the main burden for this advancement is that we have to make coding
a little more detailed, and extend the learning curve.

]Ah, for the good old days!

warren Simmons New Jersey
```

>
>In its time the '286 was the cat's pajamas.

The '286 was seriously flawed by its inability to 'thunk' between modes. It
required a General Reset to switch from protected mode to real mode.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T02:21:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0406030121.7637ca96@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406011106.2e64ce66@posting.google.com> <kX4vc.17946$Tn6.11443@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <217e491a.0406011844.2feaba8b@posting.google.com> <eUkvc.18334$eH1.8236490@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <40be8374.242987844@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> The '286 was seriously flawed by its inability to 'thunk' between modes. It
> required a General Reset to switch from protected mode to real mode.

Actually it was quite the other way around.  It was _designed_ to run
in protected mode.  If a program could defeat the protection mode by
merely switching back to real mode then there would be no point in
having protection.  It was supposed to require a general reset to get
back to real mode once it had been set (it started in real mode).

However, a design flaw did allow switching back to real mode using the
keyboard controller to maintain state.  This flaw made the chip
halfway useful.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T23:31:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40bf9c23.314841031@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406011106.2e64ce66@posting.google.com> <kX4vc.17946$Tn6.11443@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <217e491a.0406011844.2feaba8b@posting.google.com> <eUkvc.18334$eH1.8236490@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <40be8374.242987844@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406030121.7637ca96@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>having protection.  

How did designers think the operating system would run DOS programs? With a
protected mode extender? It appears they just weren't thinking.

A crude but simple solution would have been prohibiting access to memory above
1M in real mode. Extended memory drivers would have to be protected mode and
authorized by the OS.

>However, a design flaw did allow switching back to real mode using the
>keyboard controller to maintain state.  This flaw made the chip
>halfway useful.

Very good.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-06-04T00:01:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0406032301.bef31db@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406011106.2e64ce66@posting.google.com> <kX4vc.17946$Tn6.11443@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <217e491a.0406011844.2feaba8b@posting.google.com> <eUkvc.18334$eH1.8236490@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <40be8374.242987844@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406030121.7637ca96@posting.google.com> <40bf9c23.314841031@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >Actually it was quite the other way around.  It was _designed_ to run
> >in protected mode.  If a program could defeat the protection mode by
> >merely switching back to real mode then there would be no point in
> >having protection.  
 
> How did designers think the operating system would run DOS programs? With a
> protected mode extender? It appears they just weren't thinking.

The 80286 starts in real mode so there is no problem using it merely
as a faster 8086.  For protected mode environments such as Unix 286 (I
still have one of these), Xenix 286, QNX, FlexOS 286, etc, there is no
requirement to run 'DOS programs'.  Programs compiled for 286 in these
environments work well.

The 286 was designed before DOS programs 'broke' how Intel processor
_should_ be used.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia.

""The Intel 80286 (officially named the iAPX 286) is a x86 16-bit
central processing unit that was introduced by Intel on the 1st of
February, 1982.""

MS-DOS 4.0 and 4.1 (not to be confused with the much later 4.01) was a
multi-tasking DOS designed to be moved to the 80286.  The programming
guides mentioned how to write code that was compatible with both CPUs
so that it could simply be recompiled.

But it was too late, DOS programmers and compiler writers (incuding
MS) were addicted to segment calculations, address normalisation,
direct screen writes, and many other things that could not work on the
286.  Even the ability to force real mode only allowed a single DOS
box in OS/2 2.x and Windows 'standard (286) mode', and this was so
slow that it was called the penalty box.

> A crude but simple solution would have been prohibiting access to memory above
> 1M in real mode. Extended memory drivers would have to be protected mode and
> authorized by the OS.

Geez, it sounds like you should have run the Intel design team.

Actually, real mode cannot access above 1 Mb, well actually it can
access almost 64Kb above this by using segment FFFFFFFF, as long as
the A20 line doesn't wrap this around and overwrite the bottom of
memory.  The 64Kb - 16bytes allows data to be passed between real mode
and protected mode - another exploit of a design flaw.

If MS-DOS had not been such a poor design (eg very slow screen output,
poor printer and serial support) then programs would not have had to
resort to banging the hardware.  If they had been written better they
could have been successfully recompiled to protected mode as 8086 Unix
programs were.

You can't blame Intel designers for failing to predict the DOS abuse.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-06T01:51:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40c26b26.131121597@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406011106.2e64ce66@posting.google.com> <kX4vc.17946$Tn6.11443@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <217e491a.0406011844.2feaba8b@posting.google.com> <eUkvc.18334$eH1.8236490@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <40be8374.242987844@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406030121.7637ca96@posting.google.com> <40bf9c23.314841031@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406032301.bef31db@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

>> How did designers think the operating system would run DOS programs? With a
>> protected mode extender? It appears they just weren't thinking.
>
>The 80286 starts in real mode so there is no problem using it merely
>as a faster 8086.

Rebooting to run a DOS program is not "mere", it is an unacceptable delay.

> For protected mode environments such as Unix 286 (I
>still have one of these), Xenix 286, QNX, FlexOS 286, etc, there is no
>requirement to run 'DOS programs'.  Programs compiled for 286 in these
>environments work well.

No doubt. What about the other 98% of computer users?

>The 286 was designed before DOS programs 'broke' how Intel processor
>_should_ be used.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>slow that it was called the penalty box.

It wasn't slow, they just made that up. Why would it be slow?

I thought MS-DOS 4.0 was the much maligned IBM version. It was flawed but it was
not multi-tasking. The only multi-tasking DOS I ever saw was DoubleDOS, which
ran two tasks .. poorly. 

I was one of those 'addicted' DOS programmers. I wrote published screen
accelerators and async drivers.  It seems like a hundred years ago.

>> A crude but simple solution would have been prohibiting access to memory
>>above  1M in real mode. Extended memory drivers would have to be protected
>>mode and  authorized by the OS.
>
>Geez, it sounds like you should have run the Intel design team.

<Brushing fingernails on lapel.>

>Actually, real mode cannot access above 1 Mb, well actually it can
>access almost 64Kb above this by using segment FFFFFFFF, as long as
>the A20 line doesn't wrap this around and overwrite the bottom of
>memory.  The 64Kb - 16bytes allows data to be passed between real mode
>and protected mode - another exploit of a design flaw.

Correct. EMS did it with 'offboard' memory, the need for which disappeared when
the '286 appeared. EMM simulated EMS by mapping high memory into a low memory
segment.

>If MS-DOS had not been such a poor design (eg very slow screen output,
>poor printer and serial support) then programs would not have had to
>resort to banging the hardware.  If they had been written better they
>could have been successfully recompiled to protected mode as 8086 Unix
>programs were.

I had twelve fun-filled years banging the hardware in assembly language. They
were the best I ever had. Consider that DOS ruled from 1982 to 1995, fourteen
years; Windows/NT (excluding Win3) has been with us less than ten years. Toward
the end of the DOS era, I learned to bang the hardware in Cobol rather than
assembly language.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-06T00:28:56-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
