- **References:** `<40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406011106.2e64ce66@posting.google.com> <kX4vc.17946$Tn6.11443@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <217e491a.0406011844.2feaba8b@posting.google.com> <eUkvc.18334$eH1.8236490@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <40be8374.242987844@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406030121.7637ca96@posting.google.com> <40bf9c23.314841031@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406032301.bef31db@posting.google.com> <40c26b26.131121597@news.optonline.net>`

```
On Sun, 06 Jun 2004 01:51:30 GMT robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
wrote:

:>riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

:>>robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

:>>> How did designers think the operating system would run DOS programs? With a
:>>> protected mode extender? It appears they just weren't thinking.

:>>The 80286 starts in real mode so there is no problem using it merely
:>>as a faster 8086.

:>Rebooting to run a DOS program is not "mere", it is an unacceptable delay.

A properly written supervisor could run a DOS application program in protected
mode. It could handle the interrupts caused by an application doing an
operation that requires ring 0 and simulate it for the ring 3 program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-06-05T22:33:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0406052133.aee4a4e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <217e491a.0406011106.2e64ce66@posting.google.com> <kX4vc.17946$Tn6.11443@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <217e491a.0406011844.2feaba8b@posting.google.com> <eUkvc.18334$eH1.8236490@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <40be8374.242987844@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406030121.7637ca96@posting.google.com> <40bf9c23.314841031@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406032301.bef31db@posting.google.com> <40c26b26.131121597@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
  
> >The 80286 starts in real mode so there is no problem using it merely
> >as a faster 8086.
> 
> Rebooting to run a DOS program is not "mere", it is an unacceptable delay.

Let me say again:  The 80286 starts in real mode.  It can be used, and
was/is used by MS-DOS as a faster 8086 in real mode.  It does not need
to be 'rebooted' to run MS-DOS when it is used as a real mode CPU.
 
> > For protected mode environments such as ... 

> No doubt. What about the other 98% of computer users?

When the 80286 was designed there was no 'other 98% of computer
users'.  There was no DOS, no Windows.  For _most_ 8086 users the
80286 was a step up to a protected environment.

> >MS-DOS 4.0 and 4.1 (not to be confused with the much later 4.01) 

> > Even the ability to force real mode only allowed a single DOS
> >box in OS/2 2.x and Windows 'standard (286) mode', and this was so
> >slow that it was called the penalty box.
> 
> It wasn't slow, they just made that up. Why would it be slow?

Switching between protected and real modes on a 80286 such as done by
OS/2 and Windows 286 was very slow due to needing to fiddle via the
keyboard controller.

Later, on an 80386, the same process was very much faster.

> >MS-DOS 4.0 and 4.1 (not to be confused with the much later 4.01) 
 
> I thought MS-DOS 4.0 was the much maligned IBM version. It was flawed but it
> was not multi-tasking. 

I had made the comment: "Not to be confused with the much later MS-DOS
4.01" (which was the much maligned 'IBM version').  You have confused
it.

MS-DOS 4.0, also known as 'European DOS', was a multi-tasking MS-DOS
based on MS-DOS 3.1.  4.1 was based on 3.2 and was multi-tasking.

This version was mainly used by Siemens, Wang and ICL, and by DataPac
in Australia.  I still have a developer's manual here. These released
it as product, but it was cancelled by IBM and MS when development of
OS/2 (also known as MS-DOS 5.0 not to be confused with the much later
MS-DOS 5).

> The only multi-tasking DOS I ever saw was DoubleDOS, which
> ran two tasks .. poorly. 

That is a limitation entirely of your 'saw' and not of any number of
'multi-tasking DOS' that were developed and used extensively, many of
which had much better performance than MS-DOS.

> I was one of those 'addicted' DOS programmers. I wrote published screen
> accelerators and async drivers.  It seems like a hundred years ago.

So, its _your_ fault that the 80286 could not be used.   ;-)

DOS was probably the worst thing that occurred to programming.  There
are still programmers who think that it is somehow 'faster' to sit in
a CPU hogging loop waiting for 'keyhit' instead of doing a blocking
read.

There are still bumb DOS programmers using Windows and Unix who will
spend their efforts trying to grab all the resources on the basis of
'what do mean "another program" ?'.  No wait, that was you with
grabbing all the CPUs.

> Correct. EMS did it with 'offboard' memory, the need for which disappeared when
> the '286 appeared. EMM simulated EMS by mapping high memory into a low memory
> segment.

The problem being that this required the memory blocks to be copied
out of and into the 64Kb area accessible to both and this crippled its
performance.

> I had twelve fun-filled years banging the hardware in assembly language. They
> were the best I ever had. Consider that DOS ruled from 1982 to 1995, fourteen
> years; Windows/NT (excluding Win3) has been with us less than ten years. Toward
> the end of the DOS era, I learned to bang the hardware in Cobol rather than
> assembly language.

No wonder you never 'saw' multi-tasking systems.  Hardware banging is
incompatible with efficient sharing of resources.  I had been using
multi-tasking and multi-user micros since 1978.  MP/M, DRX,
Concurrent-CP/M-86 through to DR-Multiuser-DOS and Real/32, as well
and Unix and Linux of course.

No wonder you thought DoubleDOS was slow.  Your programs probably used
100% CPU time when doing nothing.  You probably never even heard of
the DOS 'Despatch' function to release the current time-slice.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-06T12:46:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40c2f3e5.166134230@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <217e491a.0406011106.2e64ce66@posting.google.com> <kX4vc.17946$Tn6.11443@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <217e491a.0406011844.2feaba8b@posting.google.com> <eUkvc.18334$eH1.8236490@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <40be8374.242987844@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406030121.7637ca96@posting.google.com> <40bf9c23.314841031@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406032301.bef31db@posting.google.com> <40c26b26.131121597@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406052133.aee4a4e@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

>MS-DOS 4.0, also known as 'European DOS', was a multi-tasking MS-DOS
>based on MS-DOS 3.1.  4.1 was based on 3.2 and was multi-tasking.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>MS-DOS 5).

I had not heard of those, despite reading Dr Dobbs, etc.  I used to poke fun at
DOS 3 features that hinted at concurrency. For example, int 21-50 Read PSP of
Current Task and 21-5C Lock/Unlock File, about which I said  "For use on your
new multi-tasking DOS". I was aware of DR products, but not the MS version.

>DOS was probably the worst thing that occurred to programming.  There
>are still programmers who think that it is somehow 'faster' to sit in
>a CPU hogging loop waiting for 'keyhit' instead of doing a blocking
>read.

I wrote a TSR Manager that detected programs waiting for keyboard input, gave
the time to others until a key was hit.

>There are still dumb DOS programmers using Windows and Unix who will
>spend their efforts trying to grab all the resources on the basis of
>'what do mean "another program" ?'.  No wait, that was you with
>grabbing all the CPUs.

It's now called 'scalability', which is a Good Thing. :)

The operating system allocates CPU cycles and other resources. When application
programmers usurp that role, their misguided altruism only cripples their
application's performance.

>> Correct. EMS did it with 'offboard' memory, the need for which disappeared
when
>> the '286 appeared. EMM simulated EMS by mapping high memory into a low memory
>> segment.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>performance.

"Observers of the time noted that MicroSoft which had only single tasking
software and Intel were working pretty closely together at the time, and it was
Digital Research who was ready to storm the market with a multi-tasking Dos but
was unable to do so because of an 'I gotcha' in the i286 instruction set. Add
some paranoia to the undisputed facts and it is pretty easy to see how some
pretty nasty stories about who was doing what to whom circulated."

"At issue here was the absence of a proposed LOADALL instruction which would
have allowed return to the real mode. Intel engineers left this instruction out
on purpose, believing that once one had gone to the protected mode, it was
irresponsible to return to the real mode, and that doing so would only lead to
inherently unstable applications. The extent to which this decision was driven
by marketing decisions as contrasted to engineering decisions will never be
fullly known. The truth probably includes some of each, but although the
ultimate impact of the decision was to sabatoge Digital Research, to the extent
marketing was involved in the decision, it may well have been driven by the urge
of Intel to promote their own operating system which was a protected mode OS. "

http://www.vannattabros.com/history5.html

>No wonder you thought DoubleDOS was slow.  Your programs probably used
>100% CPU time when doing nothing.  You probably never even heard of
>the DOS 'Despatch' function to release the current time-slice.

I'm not aware of any Yield function in DOS. The closest we had was several
versions of 'read a key and don't return until you have one'. DESQview supplied
a Yleld function. My screen driver was DESQview-aware and gave the application a
gateway  its Yield function if DESQview was running.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-06-06T13:17:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0406061217.529fc781@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <217e491a.0406011844.2feaba8b@posting.google.com> <eUkvc.18334$eH1.8236490@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <40be8374.242987844@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406030121.7637ca96@posting.google.com> <40bf9c23.314841031@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406032301.bef31db@posting.google.com> <40c26b26.131121597@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406052133.aee4a4e@posting.google.com> <40c2f3e5.166134230@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> >There are still dumb DOS programmers using Windows and Unix who will
> >spend their efforts trying to grab all the resources on the basis of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> It's now called 'scalability', which is a Good Thing. :)

If it is done, as you suggested, for simple file -> print report
programs, or batch updates, which are typical Cobol workloads, then it
is just dumb DOS programmers trying, pointlessly, to grab all the
resources.
 
> The operating system allocates CPU cycles and other resources. When application
> programmers usurp that role, their misguided altruism only cripples their
> application's performance.

As you demonstrated.  Of course you didn't actuallly try it with I/O
limited tasks, such as your suggestion for 3 x file -> report, where
it would have made the whole system even slower.

> "Observers of the time noted that MicroSoft which had only single tasking
> software and Intel were working pretty closely together at the time, and it was
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> pretty nasty stories about who was doing what to whom circulated."

DR had MP/M multi-tasking/multiuser since 1978 and Concurrent-CP/M-86
multi-tasking since 1982.  The 286 could not be used because of
limitations, but DR used EEMS (eg AST RAMPage) to overcome the
limitations of EMS and EMM until the 386 came along.  EEMS did not
need memory block copying or protect-real transitions which crippled
performance and could switch 640Kb of RAM with 16 registers.

> "At issue here was the absence of a proposed LOADALL instruction which would
> have allowed return to the real mode. Intel engineers left this instruction out
> on purpose, believing that once one had gone to the protected mode, it was
> irresponsible to return to the real mode, and that doing so would only lead to
> inherently unstable applications. 

Thank you for finally accepting what I had told you.

> The extent to which this decision was driven
> by marketing decisions as contrasted to engineering decisions will never be
> fullly known. The truth probably includes some of each, but although the
> ultimate impact of the decision was to sabatoge Digital Research, 

It didn't 'sabotage' DRI at all.  DRI just used EEMS RAMPage cards on
8088s for Concurrent-DOS and built a fast 286 memory manager for
DR-DOS.  It also had FlexOS 286 for the real time market which worked
very well with the 286 processor because it would not be burdened with
crap DOS programs.

> to the extent
> marketing was involved in the decision, it may well have been driven by the urge
> of Intel to promote their own operating system which was a protected mode OS. 

Given that the design of the 286 was finalised well before MS-DOS or
DR-DOS were even thought of then the 'history' is rather naieve.

In fact the 286 design sabotaged the MS-DOS 4.x multitasking line and
the later OS/2 1.x line much more than any effect it had on DRI, but
then your 'historian' probably never even heard of those either.


> I'm not aware of any Yield function in DOS. 

Multiplex INTx2F Ax=0x1680 'Release Current Virtual-Machine Time
Slice'.  This was in Windows/286 (ie between Windows 2 and Windows
3.0) and also in MS-DOS 5 without Windows installed.  Many
multi-taskers copied it.

> The closest we had was several
> versions of 'read a key and don't return until you have one'. DESQview

I thought that you said you hadn't heard of any except DoubleDOS,
being doing some research now ?

> supplied
> a Yleld function. My screen driver was DESQview-aware and gave the application > a gateway  its Yield function if DESQview was running.

DESQView was an implementation of IBM's TopView API.  It, too, could
use a RAMPage card but was crippled when trying to use a bare 286.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-07T00:28:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40c3b1de.214774199@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <217e491a.0406011844.2feaba8b@posting.google.com> <eUkvc.18334$eH1.8236490@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <40be8374.242987844@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406030121.7637ca96@posting.google.com> <40bf9c23.314841031@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406032301.bef31db@posting.google.com> <40c26b26.131121597@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406052133.aee4a4e@posting.google.com> <40c2f3e5.166134230@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406061217.529fc781@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

>> It's now called 'scalability', which is a Good Thing. :)
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>resources.

In large-scale batch production environments, it is common practice to have the
scheduler launch multiple programs running in parallel. Report printing programs
are especially good candidates.

All major databases look for ways to split a *single query* into multiple
threads that can run in parallel.

>> "At issue here was the absence of a proposed LOADALL instruction which would
>> have allowed return to the real mode. Intel engineers left this instruction
out
>> on purpose, believing that once one had gone to the protected mode, it was
>> irresponsible to return to the real mode, and that doing so would only lead
to
>> inherently unstable applications. 
>
>Thank you for finally accepting what I had told you.

Ironically, every 286 *did* have a LOADALL instruction. It just wasn't
documented by Intel.. I know for a fact (don't ask) that copy-protection
software used it. The op-code was 0F05 and there were no operands. It loaded a
51-word table from 80:0 (absolute) containing ALL registers, including MSW (bit
0 is real/protected), GDTR, etc. One instruction does a total context switch,
including to or from real mode.

Another way to switch the 286 into real mode is to intercept the timer interrupt
(08), then force a 'double fault'. It's a lot faster than using the keyboard
controller.

>In fact the 286 design sabotaged the MS-DOS 4.x multitasking line and
>the later OS/2 1.x line much more than any effect it had on DRI, but
>then your 'historian' probably never even heard of those either.

In fact, MS used LOADALL in  VDISK, RAMDrive and Himem.sys, which were written
before Windows. Therefore, they 'might' have used it in MS-DOS 4.x and Windows,
and they 'might' have made up an entertaining cover story about discovering the
keyboard controller at the last minute.

'We were about to give up when Joe, our genius programmer, discovered a
little-known feature in the keyboard controller. Yeah, Joe.' The rubes bought
it.

>> I'm not aware of any Yield function in DOS. 
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>multi-taskers copied it.

After my time. My notes show nothing for 2F-16xx.

>> The closest we had was several
>> versions of 'read a key and don't return until you have one'. DESQview
>
>I thought that you said you hadn't heard of any except DoubleDOS,
>being doing some research now ?

Searching my old code for 'yield' found the DV feature. I didn't regard DV as an
operating system, nor use it. Some clients did, so I made my software DV-aware.

One multi-tasking OS I knew about and forgot to mention was GEM. The OS I wrote
in 1990-92 was 'inspired' by some of its features. 

Why write a real mode OS in 1990? Because Chips & Technologies made a chip
called the 8680 that contained a whole PC -- CPU, serial ports, display adaptor,
keyboard controller, about 3M of memory. It was CMOS, ran 100 hours on AA
batteries and sold in quantity for well under $50. It was perfect for a handheld
(PDA). There was one problem .. it was 16-bit, real mode only. It couldn't run
Win CE. It cried for a GUI, multi-tasking, real mode operating system and the
obvious one, GEM, didn't seem interested. 

I worked for a company that had written half the software in the Sharp Wizard
and Casio BOSS, which ran on horrid overgrown calculator chips. We were seen as
handheld experts. When the next generation of handhelds was on the horizon, we
had the bona fides to create a specialized operating system. A US Dow-30 company
known for photography and film had already spent $1M on handwriting recognition
(which ran on a separate DSP). I worked on their operating system for two years,
18 hours a day. Then the sponsor cancelled the project, along with 100 others,
for business reasons. 

That generation of handhelds turned out to be a dud, mostly because Sharp and
Casio priced them at $600 (manufacturing cost was less than $100). Years later,
Palm succeeded with machines retailing for $200.

What does this have to do with Cobol? Although my OS kernel was written in
assembly language, most of my mockup applications and tools were written in
Cobol and compiled with Realia. Had the aspects been favorable, today's
calculators and calendars might have been Cobol code.

>DESQView was an implementation of IBM's TopView API.  It, too, could
>use a RAMPage card but was crippled when trying to use a bare 286.

Quarterdeck wrote the best-ever real mode memory manager, QEMM, which competed
with our Lightning. I think it's safe to speculate they used LOADALL on the 286.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-06-06T23:31:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0406062231.682a63db@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40be8374.242987844@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406030121.7637ca96@posting.google.com> <40bf9c23.314841031@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406032301.bef31db@posting.google.com> <40c26b26.131121597@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406052133.aee4a4e@posting.google.com> <40c2f3e5.166134230@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406061217.529fc781@posting.google.com> <40c3b1de.214774199@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> >> It's now called 'scalability', which is a Good Thing. :)

> In large-scale batch production environments, it is common practice to have the
> scheduler launch multiple programs running in parallel. Report printing programs
> are especially good candidates.

Exactly.  That is why one does _NOT_ want one of these grabbing every
CPU available, as you seem to want to do, especially when it make the
program run slower and use more total CPU time.  Given that the
scheduler will start several programs there is no gain in usurping the
scheduler's decisions by undermining the CPU loading estimates.
 
> All major databases look for ways to split a *single query* into multiple
> threads that can run in parallel.

You seem to have difficulty distinguishing between 'simple file ->
report' programs and database systems that may be the only server
program running on a multi CPU server machine.

Doing multiple concurrent 'READ NEXT' is _NOT_ useful.

> After my time. My notes show nothing for 2F-16xx.

Was Windows 3.0 'after your time' ?
 
> One multi-tasking OS I knew about and forgot to mention was GEM. The OS I 
> wrote
> in 1990-92 was 'inspired' by some of its features. 

Interesting that you think that DesqView wasn't an 'OS' yet GEM was 'a
multi-tasking OS'

GEM was neither an OS nor multi-tasking.  It ran on top of MS-DOS (or,
later, DR-DOS) or on Atari and while it was better than Windows 1
(which it pre-dated by a year or so) it was just as much a
co-operative library as Windows 1 was.  It only catered for having 8
windows total, of which 3 were 'applet' windows.

There _was_ a multi-tasking GEM called X-GEM, but it only ran on DRI's
FlexOS-386 and was not released.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-07T23:44:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40c4fd12.299575463@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40be8374.242987844@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406030121.7637ca96@posting.google.com> <40bf9c23.314841031@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406032301.bef31db@posting.google.com> <40c26b26.131121597@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406052133.aee4a4e@posting.google.com> <40c2f3e5.166134230@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406061217.529fc781@posting.google.com> <40c3b1de.214774199@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406062231.682a63db@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

>Was Windows 3.0 'after your time' ?

No. I avoided it Win 1 through 3 because they didn't look and feel (to me) like
a real OS. They fealt amateurish, like shareware. Win 95 WAS a real OS.

>Interesting that you think that DesqView wasn't an 'OS' yet GEM was 'a
>multi-tasking OS'
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>windows total, of which 3 were 'applet' windows.

As I said, I wasn't into DESQView. Given that it had a Yield function, it was
obviously multi-tasking. But that didn't mean much where there was only one
screen and that screen was text mode. Usable multi-tasking requires resizable
GUI windows, except special cases such as BBS which had multiple external
screens. DESQView supported GUI only toward the end of its life, and then only
in full-screen mode.

GEM did have multiple GUI windows. That the background ones weren't running
escaped me. One could focus on a window with a single mouse click.

Unfortunately GEM was developed by Gary Kildall, CEO of DRI. Of all the ego
maniacs we've been burdened with, namely Watson, Gerstner, Gates, Kahn, Grove
and Ellison, Gary was in a class by himself. His talent for missed opportunity
was singular. He'd piss everyone off, then try to sell a product no one wanted
to buy. Even his death in 1994 was ambiguous about the cause.
```

```
You seem to be under some impression that multitasking requires a GUI,
except in some special cases.
I would point you to OS/390, z/OS, z/VM, VM/ESA, and especially UNIX to
disabuse that notion.
None of which have the particular concept of 'yield' in them, which is
something dreamed up by those
who thought up cooperative multitasking.
```

```
>except in some special cases.
>I would point you to OS/390, z/OS, z/VM, VM/ESA, and especially UNIX to

The operating systems you cited use multiple text-mode screens (terminals).

>something dreamed up by those
>who thought up cooperative multitasking.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-06-08T14:17:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0406081317.2b62acc1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40c26b26.131121597@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406052133.aee4a4e@posting.google.com> <40c2f3e5.166134230@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406061217.529fc781@posting.google.com> <40c3b1de.214774199@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406062231.682a63db@posting.google.com> <40c4fd12.299575463@news.optonline.net> <ic7xc.584$TJ6.436@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <40c55fe8.324880874@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> I said multi-tasking requires either multiple (GUI) windows OR multiple screens.

No you didn't.  You were quite specific with:

>>> Usable multi-tasking requires resizable GUI windows, 

There was no optional on 'GUI', there was no OR, though you did have:

>>> except special cases such as BBS which had multiple external
screens.

Many BBS systems were _not_ multi-tasking at all, but were multi-user
by having an input queue and dealing with messages one after another. 
Some even ran on CP/M on a Z80.  Each user only had one session, so
that doesn't count as multi-tasking.

This looks like it might be another of your endless 'I meant'
backtracking as you learn things you never saw before.

> The operating systems you cited use multiple text-mode screens (terminals).

Multi-user.  You probably never even thought of having paged mode
terminals that support multi-tasking on a dumb text-mode screen with
instant task switching.  Since 1984 I have used and installed
multi-tasking on multiuser machines with typically 4 sessions
available instantly by keystroke at each terminal.  Even accountants
could use it.  Some terminals did allow switching between full screen
sessions and windowed overlapping sessions, but this was seldom used.

> >None of which have the particular concept of 'yield' in them, which is
> >something dreamed up by those
> >who thought up cooperative multitasking.
> 
> That's not true. They all have a Yield function.

'Yield' is a specific Windows mechanism.  Real multi-tasking has
'despatch'.
```

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>> I said multi-tasking requires either multiple (GUI) windows OR multiple
screens.
>
>No you didn't.  You were quite specific with:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

You're right. I didn't describe switching between virtual text-mode screens.

> Some terminals did allow switching between full screen
>sessions and windowed overlapping sessions, but this was seldom used.

Now that we have 17 inch monitors, I always use windowed sessions. It's easier
to switch focus by clicking on an underlying screen.

>'Yield' is a specific Windows mechanism.  Real multi-tasking has
>'despatch'.

Despatch is UK terminology. Do a search on "unix man page yield".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-06-07T23:54:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0406072254.57aac2a0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bf9c23.314841031@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406032301.bef31db@posting.google.com> <40c26b26.131121597@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406052133.aee4a4e@posting.google.com> <40c2f3e5.166134230@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406061217.529fc781@posting.google.com> <40c3b1de.214774199@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406062231.682a63db@posting.google.com> <40c4fd12.299575463@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> Usable multi-tasking requires resizable GUI windows, 

No. Completely wrong.  I still have a 'Multiuser-DOS' machine
(actually Real32) which I use for much of my work because it does
exactly what I want it to do. It has 8 full screen virtual screens
which _instantly_ switch (ie it can switch between screens faster than
I can toggle the keys that do this).  This is the last of the line
that I have used for 20 years but it still has a large place in my
working set.

While you were staring at the compiler grinding away, I was switching
it into background and doing useful work with several programs ready
waiting for me.

Even Unix has had multiple virtual consoles for 20 years (at least the
ones that I had have).

> except special cases such as BBS which had multiple external screens. 

Multi-user.

> DESQView supported GUI only toward the end of its life, and then only
> in full-screen mode.

Shrug - Multiuser-DOS could run Windows 3.11 as one task and GEM as
another or have more than one of each and still have instant switching
to several text mode.

> Unfortunately GEM was developed by Gary Kildall, CEO of DRI. Of all the ego
> maniacs we've been burdened with, namely Watson, Gerstner, Gates, Kahn, Grove
> and Ellison, Gary was in a class by himself. His talent for missed opportunity
> was singular. He'd piss everyone off, then try to sell a product no one wanted

You read far too many clueless web sites.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T02:15:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40be532b.230624248@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406011106.2e64ce66@posting.google.com> <kX4vc.17946$Tn6.11443@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <217e491a.0406011844.2feaba8b@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Back in the 60s code was typically 'self-modifying' in that the code
>segment would be directly changed during program execution, for
>example by an ALTER or by a PERFORM changing code at the EXIT to cause
>a return.

Generated code I saw in the 60s and 70s did not modify procedural code. The
compiler generated a pointer in the data division. Alters and Performs modified
it. Gotos and returns used it. 

>ie the code segment was not 'read only', this segment could not be
>shared or re-entered in any way.

Right. CICS, IMS and other transaction processors developed in the late 60s
wouldn't have worked.

>By having a separate PERFORM stack and redirected ALTERs the code
>could be generated so it was not written to and thus could be shared
>by separate tasks. 

The mainframe code I saw did not use a stack; it used dedicated pointers, one
per exit point. That's why recursion was not allowed.

>Within a single run-unit a program is reentrant if the _same_ task can
>enter it when it is still active.  For example, within one run-unit,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>there be a separate PERFORM stack and ALTER table (or no ALTER).

All true, but you left out the two most important requirements of reentrancy:

..A new call stack must be created for each instance, and dropped when it exits.
..A locking mechanism to prevent concurrent writes to Working-Storage.

>Recursive is self-reentrant in that it directly or indirectly
>re-enters itself (whereas A -> B -> A-dash or even A -> A-dash is
>re-entrant but not recursive).

Recursive is a special case because there is usually only one thread of control.
Therefore, it is not necessary to create a new call stack per instance. All
that's required is a new L-S area on a single stack.

>In the case of DLLs or shared objects (.so) these may be activated by
>different run-units.  A simple DLL may only have one data area and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>reentrancy.

That depends on the OS mechanism used -- multi-tasking or multithreading. The
Cobol definition of 'run unit' seems to be confusing this discussion more than
helping, so I'll revert to the OS mechanism, where tangible results can be
demonstrated. 

Major databases all (to my knowledge) use multithreading for parallel
operations. If two queries, coming from two users, are being processed in
parallel and both call the same Cobol program, both threads share a single
Working-Storage. 

Other platforms, SAP is a good example, use multi-tasking for parallel
operations. Under that mechanism, two queries, coming from two users, being
processed in parallel and calling the same Cobol program will fork two OS
processes. There will be one copy of procedural code in memory but each process
will have its own Working-Storage. Even if the programs were compiled with the
reentranat option. As the Standard says, there is no way to communicate between
them except via files. If there is a need to communicate, we do it outside the
Cobol domain with shared memory. 

The examples show two seemingly identical processes that play under different
rules depending on the OS mechanism, which is outside the scope of Cobol. The
calls and called programs could hypothetically be identical, yet one is defined
by Cobol as a single run unit and the other as two run units.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-01T21:52:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40bcf948.142046767@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406011106.2e64ce66@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
>> Examples of reentrant programs are DB2 Stored Procedures and plain-vanilla
CICS
>> programs, which load but one copy of the Procedure Division and permit
parallel
>> execution of multiple transactions and queries. For years, IBM has been
giving
>> each invocation its own initialized Working-Storage, which, in effect, turns
it
>> into Local-Storage. It does the same for programs CALLed by those main
programs.
>
>What you descibe as an 'example of reentrant' is not reentrant at all.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>shares read-only segments between tasks.

A reentrant subprogram is a subprogram which can be in invoked any number of
times in parallel, without interference between the various invocations. See
section A Pure (Reentrant) Parser. 
www.gnu.org/manual/bison-1.25/html_chapter/bison_14.html

The ability of code to process multiple interleaved requests for service nearly
simultaneously. For example, a reentrant function can begin responding to one
call, be interrupted by other calls, and complete them all with the same results
as if the function had received and executed each call serially. 
developer.apple.com/techpubs/macosx/Essentials/SystemOverview/Glossary/chapter_17_section_1.html


A characteristic of a function which can be called concurrently by multiple
threads without mishap. 
www.ccsm.ucar.edu/models/cpl6/docs/cpl6_reqdoc/node5.html

The attribute of a program or routine that allows the same copy of a program or
routine to be used concurrently by two or more tasks. 
www.cascv.brown.edu/compute/cxxmanual/glossary/r.htm

A term used to describe routines which do not modify themselves or global
variables. 
www.rtems.com/rtems/releases/4.5.0/rtemsdoc-4.5.0/share/rtemsdoc/html/c_user/c_user00412.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-06-01T20:35:40-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0406011935.4c820b4f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406011106.2e64ce66@posting.google.com> <40bcf948.142046767@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >> For years, IBM has been giving
> >> each invocation its own initialized Working-Storage, which, in effect,
> >> turns it
> >> into Local-Storage. It does the same for programs CALLed by those main
>  programs.

> >What you descibe as an 'example of reentrant' is not reentrant at all.
> > It is just a shared code segment between separate tasks.  

> A reentrant subprogram is a subprogram which can be in invoked any number of
> times in parallel, without interference between the various invocations. 

What you described was exactly how separate run-units are created. 
Each program is given its own W-S and each called program from that
also has its own W-S.

If the code segments of those programs are read-only (ie not
self-modifying code) then it is an option of the operating system (or
sub-system) whether a fresh copy is loaded or it merely reuses the one
already in the virtual address space.

Now I can 'invoke' the same program several times on my multi-user
machine, in fact I do that all the time, and these are 'without
interference between them', except at the record locks.

This means that _absolutely_everything_ is 'reentrant' by that
definition.

> The attribute of a program or routine that allows the same copy of a program 
> or routine to be used concurrently by two or more tasks. 

This one includes two important distinctions:  'the same copy of a
program'. A _program_ is more than just the code segment, it also
include the related data items. 'The same copy' also refers to the
program's data.

A re-entrant code segment is just a read-only shared block of code,
but is not a program.  A re-entrant program does, as you say, allow it
to be used concurrently.  This may require that it lock data items to
prevent concurrent reuse, or it may set up separate and distinct data
areas for each invocation, or at least for each task.

A Cobol DLL or .so for example may have a shared data segment to hold
constants and initial values for W-S and will create a new W-S for
each task that calls it, and will probably use the caller's stack.

If this is how the system has implemented DLLs then the DLL library
will be re-entrant by separate tasks even if the Cobol program within
it is not re-entrant in Cobol terms.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programs expecting "initial values"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T02:15:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40be5d49.233214781@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<A0Nuc.16946$Tn6.14670@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40bbe62f.71610928@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406011106.2e64ce66@posting.google.com> <40bcf948.142046767@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406011935.4c820b4f@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>also has its own W-S.

That's an implementation detail. On Micro Focus, reentrant(2) does give each
program its own W-S whereas reentrant(1) does not. As I pointed out in a
previous contribution to this thread, the operating system gives each program
its own W-S if the program is running as a task but does not if the program is
running as a thread.

>If the code segments of those programs are read-only (ie not
>self-modifying code) then it is an option of the operating system (or
>sub-system) whether a fresh copy is loaded or it merely reuses the one
>already in the virtual address space.

Why would it load a fresh copy? Wait .. I know .. to sell more memory on your
MVS machine.

>Now I can 'invoke' the same program several times on my multi-user
>machine, in fact I do that all the time, and these are 'without
>interference between them', except at the record locks.

Passing transient data through files or databases is clumsy. Why not use shared
memory or pipes? For example, a request to start a long-running batch-like
program.

>This means that _absolutely_everything_ is 'reentrant' by that
>definition.

You're right. If every program gets its own Data Division and call stack, two or
more copies can run in parallel. We've been doing it with batch programs for 50
years.

>> The attribute of a program or routine that allows the same copy of a program 
>> or routine to be used concurrently by two or more tasks. 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>areas for each invocation, or at least for each task.

That depends on the definition of "task". If a task is an OS process, the most
common usage, the definition of reentrancy is incorrect in re Cobol, which does
not natively support shared memory. If a task is a thread, sloppy usage IMO, the
definition applies.
```

```
dateline from 23:45 Thursday 12 March (GMT-1200) to 00:45 Saturday 14
(NZ Summer time is GMT+1300).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: big whoops

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T09:07:49-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-7A6313.09074802062004@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<40b686c2.441959863@news.optonline.net> <40b693ba.445280025@news.optonline.net> <c96sn5$svf$1@panix5.panix.com> <40b7bbd0.4583667@news.optonline.net> <c9a3lu$ckt$1@panix5.panix.com> <40bbe9ec.72568167@news.optonline.net> <joe_zitzelberger-EB8379.07154501062004@corp.supernews.com> <40bd28f0.154247846@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <40bd28f0.154247846@news.optonline.net>,
 robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

It has nothing to do with the government.  It has everything to do with 
the timezone where the reporter talked to someone and/or filed the story.

The difference you cite between notifying Bergs parents and the official 
time of notification could well be the exact same instant in time, just 
on different days.  E.g. FBI-Bagdad local time and Berg parent local 
time.

When you start citing several sources for the dates it gets murky.  The 
military likely used Bagdad local time.  Unless the time of the press 
release from the pentagon was used, in which case it would be Washington 
local time.  I'm almost certain that Bergs parents use their local time, 
EST I believe.  While I have no idea what the FBI used.
```

```
Gitmo isn't working if it takes months.
```

## Thread: win98 to XP — 2 this month / 20 total

_Thread started [2004-05](2004-05.md#t-adab51e701)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: win98 to XP

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-06-01T16:54:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c9icbl02qlt@news2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<6N8tc.30950$sr3.867608@news20.bellglobal.com> <EtmdnQUnmYiGjyTdRVn-hQ@comcast.com> <XDjuc.42$Hn.13500@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

In article <XDjuc.42$Hn.13500@news20.bellglobal.com>, Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> writes:
> > "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
> > news:6N8tc.30950$sr3.867608@news20.bellglobal.com...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> simply installed the runtime.

Does the program do a lot of file I/O?  Maybe a virus checker or the
XP Indexing Service or something else that hooks the filesystem is
unhappy with its I/O access pattern.  I'd try disabling those to see
if it makes a difference.

(I had a similar problem once with the freeware Fundelete utility,
which extends the Windows "Recycle Bin" undelete mechanism to all
file deletions - command line, programmatic, etc.  It worked fine
for normal use, but slowed the Norton Antivirus system scan down
by a couple of orders of magnitude.  Apparently NAV does a lot of
temporary file creation and deletion, probably when checking locked
files, at least in that version.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: win98 to XP

## Thread: Ree: Alternative COBOL "telco" source program — 7 this month / 8 total

_Thread started [2004-05](2004-05.md#t-321b88b19e)._

#### ↳ Re: Ree: Alternative COBOL "telco" source program

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T01:22:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40bd1278.148495052@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0405312118.6c36def2@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>>robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>won't get A's data, nor will any new run-unit.

That depends on which compiler option was used. In the case of Micro Focus'
reentrant(1), which I demonstrated here in a working program, W-S is shared
between instances. They are expected to obtain a lock before writing to W-S.

If a new W-S is created for each instance, as you claim, why was it necessary to
invent L-S? 

>> It seems pointless to make [pic 1] default to display. 
>

>If PIC 1 is computational in nature then a MOVE [PIC 1(8) COMP] 
>TO [PIC 1(8) DISPLAY] may unpack the bits to separate bytes.  PIC 9
>won't do this.

Sure it will.

01  A pic 9(2) binary-byte unsigned value x'AA'.
01  B pic 9(8) display.

compute B = A * 11111110
if B equal to 10101010
   display 'it worked'
else
   display 'foiled again ' B
end-if
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Ree: Alternative COBOL "telco" source program

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-06-01T23:35:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0406012235.40ccb973@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0405312118.6c36def2@posting.google.com> <40bd1278.148495052@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >The question was "_and_gets_your_data_".
 
> That depends on which compiler option was used. In the case of Micro Focus'
> reentrant(1), which I demonstrated here in a working program, W-S is shared
> between instances. They are expected to obtain a lock before writing to W-S.

You seem to fail to notice a difference between 'instances', which are
_within_ a run-unit, and run-units, which are completely indepenent.

In the case of an 'abend' the whole run-unit disappears and there is
nothing that can 'get your data' as you claimed.
 
> If a new W-S is created for each instance, as you claim, why was it necessary > to invent L-S? 

You are confused.  Within a single run-unit two instances of a program
will share one W-S but will have a separate L-S (ie reentrant(1) or
lack of reentrant(n)).  The new 'reentrant(2)' (as I understand it)
means that two instances within one run-unit will not share W-S but
will each get a new one - in which case L-S may be redundant for those
programs.

Two separate run-units (tasks) will have W-S that is _not_ shared with
any other run-unit regardless of any 'reentrant(n)', nor if one
run-unit abends will the other get access to the data of the one that
abended (abent?).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ree: Alternative COBOL "telco" source program

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T02:15:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40be87e6.244125700@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0405312118.6c36def2@posting.google.com> <40bd1278.148495052@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406012235.40ccb973@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>_within_ a run-unit, and run-units, which are completely indepenent.

If I failed to notice, let me rectify -- multithreading within a "run unit"
shares Working-Storage; multi-tasking does not.

>In the case of an 'abend' the whole run-unit disappears and there is
>nothing that can 'get your data' as you claimed.

Right, unless you use IPC or similar shared memory. There is no reason to use
IPC in a multitheaded program. It is commonly used in multi-tasked programs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ree: Alternative COBOL "telco" source program

```
Since, in all parts of the world *I* know of, IPC stands for Inter Process Communications,
you darn well need to use it in multithreaded programs. Mutexs are the primary means
of synchronizing data access for threads.
```

```
Working-Storage, not IPC.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ree: Alternative COBOL "telco" source program

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T11:42:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<A8Evc.4969$bt4.2750@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0405312118.6c36def2@posting.google.com> <40bd1278.148495052@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406012235.40ccb973@posting.google.com> <40be87e6.244125700@news.optonline.net> <eryvc.29189$oh7.21904@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`

```
"Paul Raulersonv" <pkrauleson@verizon.net> wrote in message
news:eryvc.29189$oh7.21904@nwrddc01.gnilink.net...
> Since, in all parts of the world *I* know of, IPC stands for Inter Process
Communications,
> you darn well need to use it in multithreaded programs. Mutexs are the
primary means
> of synchronizing data access for threads.

Except in any parts of the world using the Windows(r) Operating system.

Under Windows, multi-threading means intra-process, so inter-process
techniques are moot; and the primary method of data synchronization across
threads of a process is the Critical Section, although a mutex can be used
if desired.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ree: Alternative COBOL "telco" source program

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T10:17:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c9nmer$a3r$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0405312118.6c36def2@posting.google.com> <40bd1278.148495052@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0406012235.40ccb973@posting.google.com> <40be87e6.244125700@news.optonline.net> <eryvc.29189$oh7.21904@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`

```
"Paul Raulersonv" <pkrauleson@verizon.net> wrote in message
news:eryvc.29189$oh7.21904@nwrddc01.gnilink.net...

> Since, in all parts of the world *I* know of, IPC stands for Inter Process
Communications,

Ummm ... Really?

ANSI X3.23-1974 explicitly defines "IPC" as an abbreviation for
"Inter-Program Communication" (not "Inter-Process Communication", with or
without the hyphen) on Page I-8 in 1.8, SHORTHAND NOTATION.

ANSI X3.23-1985 does the same on page I-4 in 1.4, HOW TO USE THE STANDARD.

ISO/IEC 1989:2002 uses the expanded term "inter-program communication"
throughout, avoiding the abbreviation; since that standard provides neither
modules nor permissible levels of implementation within those modules, there
is no need for an abbreviation for the module name; the inter-program
communication capabilities are simply part of the overall standard.  I see
neither the term nor the concept "inter-process communication" addressed in
the 2002 COBOL standard, with the possible exception of the mention of its
possibility in "A run unit is an independent entity that may be executed
without communicating with, or being coordinated with, any other run unit
except that it may process files and messages or set and test switches that
were written to or will be read by other run units." on page 385 in 14.5.1,
Run unit organization, first paragraph.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Corrupt indexed files and the "Cobfut32"

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T12:00:11-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0406021100.44384077@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<40baf740$0$1794$a729d347@news.telepac.pt> <P%Fuc.6546$Hn.268096@news20.bellglobal.com> <40bdb17f$0$1811$a729d347@news.telepac.pt>`

```
"TekSoft" <eute@sapo.pt> wrote 

>     We use Fujitsu PowerCobol and we don't use DECLARATIVES.
>     After opening each file we test the status, but when the file has
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>     END-IF

When testing a file status you should not test both bytes for "00",
you should only test the first byte for indicating success.  When the
first byte is '0' then the operation has been successful but the
second byte may contain additional information.

For example "02" indicates a successful read or write with additional
information that there is an allowed duplicate alternate key.

> What should we do to detect corrupted registers in the file?

When you say 'it doesn't return an error' are you saying that the
file-status is "00" and the program continues, or is it that the
program completely abends before it gets to the IF ?

Of course this is Fujitsu so the DISPLAY will occur on the 'console'. 
Has the console been suppresed or lost so that you are just not seeing
the 'ERROR' ?
```

```
JM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Corrupt indexed files and the "Cobfut32"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T11:53:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0406031053.792f34a2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<40baf740$0$1794$a729d347@news.telepac.pt> <P%Fuc.6546$Hn.268096@news20.bellglobal.com> <40bdb17f$0$1811$a729d347@news.telepac.pt> <217e491a.0406021100.44384077@posting.google.com> <40bedb88$0$1839$a729d347@news.telepac.pt>`

```
"TekSoft" <eute@sapo.pt> wrote

>     The problem is when a file have corrupted registers the return status is
> "00", therefore when we open a file we could never know if the file has or
> hasn't got corrupted registers. In MicroFocus in this same situation it
> returns a status different from "00".

At what point then do you find out that the 'registers' are corrupt ? 
Perhaps Fujitsu cannot know about the corruption when the file is
merely opened.

Microfocus, for example, will report a 9/41 error only when it
actually reads a section of corrupt index.  If a particular program
reads an area of the file and gets a 9/41 other programs may be quite
happily using the file because they don't read that area.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Corrupt indexed files and the "Cobfut32"

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T16:03:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5c2dncFtlts13SPdRVn-vA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<40baf740$0$1794$a729d347@news.telepac.pt> <P%Fuc.6546$Hn.268096@news20.bellglobal.com> <40bdb17f$0$1811$a729d347@news.telepac.pt>`

```
TekSoft wrote:
> Hi,
>     We use Fujitsu PowerCobol and we don't use DECLARATIVES.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> JM

1. Without DECLARATIVES, the above code will generate an on-screen error
message UNLESS you've put an entry in the COBOL85.CBR file to suppress them.
In other words, the compiler puts scary stuff on the screen BEFORE it
processes your file-status code.

2. You can check the internals of a file via:

   CALL 'CFURCOV'
    USING BY REFERENCE WSIDISAM
    BY VALUE WSINDX
    BY REFERENCE WSMESSAGE-AREA
    RETURNING WSRETURN-VALUE.
```

<a id="t-343aa820c6"></a>
## Thread: cobol compiler for aix? — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### cobol compiler for aix?

- **From:** Mike <mikee@mikee.ath.cx>
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T12:15:24
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10brh6s2arfgke5@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I'm quite accustomed to C, but not COBOL. What is a good, reliable
COBOL compiler for use on AIX? Is there any advantage to using an
open source compiler that translates COBOL to C, then compiles the
C with gcc?

The possible project is converting off our VSE mainframe and VSAM
to AIX and either DB2 or Oracle.

Mike
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol compiler for aix?

- **From:** "Gary" <IDontThink@so.com.so>
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T15:46:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QDmvc.36060$js4.36054@attbi_s51>`
- **References:** `<10brh6s2arfgke5@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Mike" <mikee@mikee.ath.cx> wrote:
> I'm quite accustomed to C, but not COBOL. What is a good, reliable
> COBOL compiler for use on AIX? Is there any advantage to using an
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> to AIX and either DB2 or Oracle.

Hi Mike.

You could convert the COBOL to C, but my experience with high-level language
conversions is that they do work (to a point), but what you're left with is
difficult to maintain. Primarily because the resulting code conforms to some
"language conversion" methodology, and not to any "structure programming"
practice best suited for the target language.

Difficult to tell for sure, but I expect your application will migrate
relatively easily. You can target a COBOL/ISAM platform on AIX before moving
the data over to a relational db.

These links should be useful (if only to move your thoughts forward):

http://www.microfocus.com/solutions/migrate/ibm/
http://www.microfocus.com/products/serverexpress/datasheet.asp
http://supportline.microfocus.com/productreleaselevels/unix.asp

Gary.
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol compiler for aix?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T19:55:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ygqvc.20801$be.14268@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<10brh6s2arfgke5@corp.supernews.com>`

```
It would seem to me that the "default" compiler to use would be IBM's COBOL Set
for AIX.  Micro Focus, RM, AcuCOBOL (and possibly Fujitsu) all sell compilers
for that environment as well.

PerCOBOL has a "C intermediate" stage - but I don't (personally) see any
advantage to that.
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol compiler for aix?

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T14:39:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0406021339.4abfeee2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<10brh6s2arfgke5@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Mike <mikee@mikee.ath.cx> wrote 

> I'm quite accustomed to C, but not COBOL. What is a good, reliable
> COBOL compiler for use on AIX? 

> Is there any advantage to using an
> open source compiler that translates COBOL to C, then compiles the
> C with gcc?

Depends on what you mean by 'advantage'.  The translator won't give
you a 'C program', but merely a 'Cobol program in an unreadable form
that gcc can deal with'.  The C code is not designed to be used by
humans.

I also doubt that OpenCOBOL and TinyCOBOL are robust and complete
enough, yet, for production work.
 
> The possible project is converting off our VSE mainframe and VSAM
> to AIX and either DB2 or Oracle.

If you are spending such huge amounts on the machine and database (not
compared to VSE of course) then it would seem poor economics to save a
couple of thousand to use a free compiler.
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol compiler for aix?

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T02:15:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40be84b9.243312547@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<10brh6s2arfgke5@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Mike <mikee@mikee.ath.cx> wrote:

>I'm quite accustomed to C, but not COBOL. What is a good, reliable
>COBOL compiler for use on AIX? Is there any advantage to using an
>open source compiler that translates COBOL to C, then compiles the
>C with gcc?

Converting mainframe to Unix is what I do for a living. Go with either IBM or
Micro Focus Cobol. 

The open source compiler sounds like Kobol. It was written to the '85 Cobol
standard without knowledge of or regard for IBM extensions widely used by VSE
programmers. You'll have lots of incompatibilities and wind up with C code
that's hard to support.

>The possible project is converting off our VSE mainframe and VSAM
>to AIX and either DB2 or Oracle.

None of the above. Informix is easier to use and faster. Although I haven't used
them, I'd seriously consider MySQL and Postgres, based on reports from happy
users.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: cobol compiler for aix?

## Thread: low-value — 49 msgs

### low-value

- **From:** Julian <whateveryousend@iwontread.net>
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T16:18:58+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c9knac$cii$1@news.brutele.be>`

```
Hi!

Short explanation better than a big one, here is the problem :

77 cadet-datenaiss	pic 9(6).
(which represents the year of birth of the youngest worker)

my file is

01 rec-contrat.
	03 rec-num-contract	pic 9(6).  (contract number)
	03 rec-nom		pic x(30). (name)
	03 rec-datenaiss 	pic 9(6). (year of birth YYMMDD)

while loading my file into a table, i would like to determine the 
youngest worker by doing so :

perform until eof = 1
	/*loading procedure*/
	if rec-datenaiss > cadet-datenaiss then move rec-datenaiss to 	 
cadet-datenaiss.
end-perform.

i set cadet-datenaiss to the lowest value, and there is my problem :
copy/paste says :
	source literal is non-numeric - substituting zero
at line :
	move low-value to cadet-datenaiss.


thanx in advance! :)
```

#### ↳ Re: low-value

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T10:38:51-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8Glvc.97000$tb4.3963716@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<c9knac$cii$1@news.brutele.be>`
- **References:** `<c9knac$cii$1@news.brutele.be>`

```
Julian wrote:
> Hi!
> 
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> thanx in advance! :)

Do not set it to "low-value", as "LOw-value" is not numeric.  Set it to 
zero, just as the compiler has done for you.  That is the lowest *LEGAL* 
value that will allow you to do date comparisons.

Low-value is NOT the "lowest legal value" in Cobol.  "Low-value" is the 
lowest possible value in the alphabetic colating sequence for a specific 
machine, and applies only *alphabetic* fields.  Numeric fields, on the 
other hand, must be legal numeric *digits* in the aplhabetic set you are 
using. The lowest legal *Number*(unsigned that is) is zero.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC A(n)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T13:08:55-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2ZIvc.111666$tb4.4350994@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<c9nd6c$m1l$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<c9knac$cii$1@news.brutele.be> <8Glvc.97000$tb4.3963716@news20.bellglobal.com> <cuKdnd_ODsFYoyPd4p2dnA@giganews.com> <jWrvc.101340$tb4.4060897@news20.bellglobal.com> <c9nd6c$m1l$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On  2-Jun-2004, Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> did you use it?

Well, I am coding experimental stuff, and I wanted an object defined as 
a logical word ... that is, a natural language word. I am playing with 
code generation.  A sentence would be defined as a string of such words, 
followed by a period.

The definition of a "word" would be a(16), no leading or imbedded 
spaces.  I could have made it X(16) and done the edits, then just left 
it, but I decided to actually use the A(16) out of a sense of purity <G>.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC A(n)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T17:44:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c9no21$ert$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<c9knac$cii$1@news.brutele.be> <8Glvc.97000$tb4.3963716@news20.bellglobal.com> <cuKdnd_ODsFYoyPd4p2dnA@giganews.com> <jWrvc.101340$tb4.4060897@news20.bellglobal.com> <c9nd6c$m1l$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <2ZIvc.111666$tb4.4350994@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

On  3-Jun-2004, Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

> The definition of a "word" would be a(16), no leading or imbedded
> spaces.  I could have made it X(16) and done the edits, then just left
> it, but I decided to actually use the A(16) out of a sense of purity <G>.

What edits would make sense to do with X(16) that aren't needed for A(16)?

The difference between A(16) and X(16) is the former can abort with
non-alphabetic data in it.   Which isn't the best reason for not doing edits.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC A(n)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T15:07:30-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cIKvc.33988$Hn.1055477@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<c9no21$ert$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<c9knac$cii$1@news.brutele.be> <8Glvc.97000$tb4.3963716@news20.bellglobal.com> <cuKdnd_ODsFYoyPd4p2dnA@giganews.com> <jWrvc.101340$tb4.4060897@news20.bellglobal.com> <c9nd6c$m1l$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <2ZIvc.111666$tb4.4350994@news20.bellglobal.com> <c9no21$ert$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On  3-Jun-2004, Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> non-alphabetic data in it.   Which isn't the best reason for not doing edits.


Screen I/O. The edits are automatic, and done on a key by key basis 
rather than waiting for an entire field to be entered.  It still will 
not catch leading spaces or embedded spaces, of course.

Donald
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T19:51:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hjLvc.487$uX2.348@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<c9knac$cii$1@news.brutele.be> <8Glvc.97000$tb4.3963716@news20.bellglobal.com> <cuKdnd_ODsFYoyPd4p2dnA@giganews.com> <jWrvc.101340$tb4.4060897@news20.bellglobal.com> <c9nd6c$m1l$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
FYI - in the 2002 Standard, the VALIDATE statement is able to use PIC A versus
PIC X to determine that something *is* alphabetic rather than alphanumeric.

History:
   While developing the 2002 Standard, there was discussion of placing PIC A in
the "archaic" (or even "obsolete" category) - because of its "lack" of
usefulness and limited real world use.  Then it was noted that the NEW feature
of VALIDATE would work better with it - than without.
```

#### ↳ Re: low-value

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T16:19:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<o6nvc.115659$hH.2031860@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<c9knac$cii$1@news.brutele.be>`

```

Julian <whateveryousend@iwontread.net> wrote in message news:c9knac$cii$1@news.brutele.be...
>
> Short explanation better than a big one, here is the problem :
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> end-perform.

You should initialize cadet-datenaiss to value zeroes.

With that said, you will still have a couple of problems.

Your comparison logic is incorrect.  You have the wrong operator.

You will end up with the date-of-birth of the oldest worker.
And an unknown one to boot, as you are not saving the name,
although it is unclear from your post as to the need to do that.

You also have a first-time-through error of omission.

You need to check cadet-datenaiss for value zeroes
and move rec-datenaiss to cadet-datenaiss
if cadet-datenaiss is equal to value zeroes,
otherwise rec-datenaiss will never be less than cadet-datenaiss.

That isn't the only way to do it.  It is ONE way to do it.
```

```
if rec-datenaiss > cadet-datenaiss then move rec-datenaiss to 
cadet-datenaiss.

the very first value of cadet-datenaiss is now 0 (as you told me) and 
entering into the loop, its value will automatically take 
rec-datenaiss from the first record. no matter when the worker has 
born, it should be "older" than 0.

> 
> You also have a first-time-through error of omission.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
I tried to nudge you towards taking a good look at your logic,
without handing it to you on a plate.  I was going to comment
on the lack of the Century in the date field,
but I didn't want to do your homework for you.
I wanted to see if YOU would notice the potential errors.
You should probably calculate the Age of the person,
and use the "less than" operator.
Again, that isn't the only way to do it.  It is ONE way to do it.

Your example will not work properly, as your file lacks the integrity
to support the requirement.

John is born on December 31, 1999.
Mary is born on December 31, 2003.

According to you, based upon what you have been given to work with
in terms of file structure, John is older than Mary because 99 is greater
than 03, which is an incorrect conclusion.

> the very first value of cadet-datenaiss is now 0 (as you told me) and
> entering into the loop, its value will automatically take
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

Remember this.  The objective of the exercise is not necessarily
to print out the correct answer.  Rather, it is much more important
that the exercise develops your powers of reasoning, and presents
you with issues which are not readily apparent, so that you develop
a mindset whereby you are always on the lookout for hidden pitfalls.

Again I will ask you.  What will your answer be if and when
your instructor asks "Who is the youngest worker".

Before you state the obvious, don't.  I already realize that,
in the real world, there will not be any workers under the age of 5.
Or 10.  Or prehaps even 15.  That isn't the point.

This isn't a real-world example.  If it was a real-world example,
you wouldn't be using 6-digit date fields.
>
> --
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: low-value

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T10:01:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c9l15l$1hlr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<c9knac$cii$1@news.brutele.be>`

```

"Julian" <whateveryousend@iwontread.net> wrote in message
news:c9knac$cii$1@news.brutele.be...

> copy/paste says :
> source literal is non-numeric - substituting zero
> at line :
> move low-value to cadet-datenaiss.

I'm pretty sure the statement "move low-value to cadet-datenaiss" is legal
according to the '74, '85 or '02 standard.

ISO/IEC 1989:2002 page 477, 14.8.24.2 MOVE statement, syntax rule 5
prohibits SPACES as a source when the destination is numeric, but does not
prohibit QUOTES, HIGH-VALUES or LOW-VALUES.  Page 480, MOVE statement,
general rule 4b (in particular 4b3b) defines the behavior for these
non-prohibited figurative constants as treating the contents of the
figurative constant, for the length of the destination, as an unsigned
integer.  Depending on what the implementation does if the resulting
"virtual unsigned integer" is not numeric, the results may or may not be
meaningful.

I'm curious as to whether the source editor is actively preventing you from
doing this, or is merely issuing an advisory.  I would not expect a syntax
error from a standards-conforming compiler on this MOVE.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: low-value

- **From:** Karl Kiesel <Karl.Kiesel@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T15:50:09+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40BF2C91.63260301@fujitsu-siemens.com>`
- **References:** `<c9knac$cii$1@news.brutele.be> <c9l15l$1hlr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens schrieb:
> I'm pretty sure the statement "move low-value to cadet-datenaiss" is legal
> according to the '74, '85 or '02 standard.

the note following general rule 5 of move statement should be
highlighted in this context:
 'NOTE MOVE of alphanumeric figurative constants to numeric items is an
archaic feature and its use should be avoided.'

Karl Kiesel
Fujitsu Siemens Computers GmbH, Mï¿½nchen
```

- **References:** `<c9knac$cii$1@news.brutele.be> <c9l4jn$at2$1@panix5.panix.com> <c9l6lv$gf7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <c9l76b$1t4$1@news.brutele.be>`

```
In article <c9l76b$1t4$1@news.brutele.be>,
Julian  <whateveryousend@iwontread.net> wrote:
>Howard Brazee wrote:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>ahahahha !! ok i do that !!!

Be careful, now... I recall, years ago, coding a rather ugly 
initialisation-loop for a badly-designed table; I commented it by saying 
something like

*** THIS MAY LOOK RATHER UGLY AND WASTEFUL BUT THE CODE IS NECESSARY
*** BECAUSE THE SILLY-HEAD WHO DESIGNED THE LAYOUT DIDN'T REALISE THAT
*** THIS STRUCTURE COULD NOT BE ADDRESSED IN A SIMPLER MANNER

... and the Project Leader, doing his end-of-the-week code review, looked 
up from the greenbar listing...

'Silly-head?'

'Silly-head... it seems to be an elegant, simple structure on the surface 
but it cannot be addressed except in this manner.'

'Silly-head?'

'Silly-head... but maybe I'm missing something, is there another way you 
can see to initialise the area?'

'*I* designed this structure.'

'Oh... so, is there another way to initialise it?'

'... no.'

(note to native English-speakers: the epithet I employed in the comments 
was actually 'chuckle-head'... but that is harder to explain 
cross-culturally)

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: low-value

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T02:38:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40be8d12.245449990@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<c9knac$cii$1@news.brutele.be> <c9l3er$e04$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <c9l4jn$at2$1@panix5.panix.com> <s2svc.101394$tb4.4064753@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

>Hell, I'm moving all my code back to two digit dates.  Faster to type. 
>Besides, I'll need the work of fixing it in my old age.

Why stop there? I worked on an Accounts Payable system that used a ONE digit
year. It was good for only ten years. 

Did we fix it by expanding the year to four digits? Of course not. Invoices were
seldom more than two years old, so we Windowed it on the year we did the fix
minus two. Not a sliding window, a hardcoded window. 

Eight years hence, another employment 'opportunity'.
```

## Thread: Printing to an ink jet printer. — 6 msgs

### Printing to an ink jet printer.

- **From:** sbelt@dpcsystems.com
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T15:40:57-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ljesb0tq4b75638ei20hp0u49vu0bkihra@4ax.com>`

```
Hello,

I hope this is not too far off topic but I'm running out of places to
look and ideas to try........................

ANY help or suggestion would be appreciated!!

We have figured out how to scan a business form, import one or create
one and then put it over on our unix server.  We can then send the
form to a Lexmark S series or T series laser printer and keep it as a
"macro".  Then we can send data to the printer and get the form with
data printed.

Has anyone been successful in doing this with an ink jet printer?

Everything we have tried or investigated says "no - it can't be done."

Any feedback will be greatly appreciated.

sbelt@dpcsystems.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Printing to an ink jet printer.

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-02T15:48:11-05:00

```

> Hello,
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> sbelt@dpcsystems.com

Just guessing, but ink-jet printers are crap and probably don't have the
memory necessary to store a form. The so-called 'WinPrinters' (you have to
be running Windows the make the sucker work) even emulate an 85 cent chip in
the Windows driver to keep the cost down.

Ink-jet printers conform to the Gillette model: "Give away the razor and
sell the blades."
```
