#### ↳ Re: Printing to an ink jet printer.

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T00:48:43-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0406022348.762976b0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ljesb0tq4b75638ei20hp0u49vu0bkihra@4ax.com>`

```
sbelt@dpcsystems.com wrote 

> We have figured out how to scan a business form, import one or create
> one and then put it over on our unix server.  We can then send the
> form to a Lexmark S series or T series laser printer and keep it as a
> "macro".  Then we can send data to the printer and get the form with
> data printed.

I do a field merge on a postscript file to get form printing.  Using
'impress' (no not the OO.o one) to draw the form putting
<!%fieldname%> where the data is to be merged, print this to
postscript file.  A program then takes a data file of the field values
and the PS file and merges the data into the fields.  There are a few
tricks to this, but is quite easy.  With Linux CUPS the output can be
sent to any printer via a ghostscript driver - directly connected, or
via Windows using smb.
```

#### ↳ Re: Printing to an ink jet printer.

- **From:** "Jim Morcombe" <jim@byronics.com.au>
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T18:00:20+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c9ms3v$2jp$1@yeppa.connect.com.au>`
- **References:** `<ljesb0tq4b75638ei20hp0u49vu0bkihra@4ax.com>`

```
I hate printers and me providing advice on them is like a blind man helping
to choose the colour of the wall paper.

I have no idea what you mean by keeping the form as a "macro" on the
printer, so I'm really totally ignorant of what you are doing.

Since this is a Cobol forum, I will assume you have some programming skills.

If I were doing it, I'd pretend I was dealing with PostScript printers.
Then once I figured out how to do it, it would then work on any postscript
printer.

If you have a look at "ghostscript", it will let you pretend that your
Lexmark printers and InkJet Printers of whatever brand are PostScript
printers.

(Half a day stuffing around downloading GhostScript, half a day to read the
documentation, 10 minutes to install and test)

So now all of your reports have to be generated as a text file full of
PostScript commands.  But since you have already written all of your reports
and they aren't in PostScript commands, what do you do?  Depends on how your
reports are produced at the moment and I don't know this?

(Whatever, this will be another day stuffing around getting everything to
come out PostScript)

So given that you have found a little utility to get all your reports into
PostScript format...

Now the problem now becomes, "How do I print a form and then print an
arbitrary report on top of it".

I would attack the Unix Printer Interface Script and add a single line of
code to it to call a filter that I would then write.

If my Form was stored as an image, then all my filter would do is to add a
single line to the front of each report with an "image" operator.  This
image would then be printed by the first "showpage" operator to come along -
and lo and behold, that will be at the end of the first page of your report.

(Half a day to read the PostScript tutorial, half a day to write and test
the shell script)

But what if there is a multi-page report?
Then my filter would need to insert the image just before each showpage.
(Change the half day to a full day to write the Shell Script)

But since I am now dealing with Postscript, I wouldn't leave the Form as an
image since its easy enough to do something better now that we are playing
with PostScript :)
(Another couple of days stuffing around, because once you start doing this
you won't be satisfied until the client tells you to stop stuffing around
and just give him what you have done so far.  He'll be happy, but you will
always wish you could have spend just 5 more minutes on it).


On the other hand, if you have absolutely no sense of pride and are willing
to deny you ever wrote the stuff in the first place, you could just forget
the PostScript stuff and...

Try attacking the printer interface script and see what happens if you throw
a few reverse linefeeds in.  If you are lucky, the printer might recognise
them and let you overprint earlier bits of the report.  In that case, you
just "cat" the form to the printer, send 66 reverse linefeeds (or whatever)
and then carry on printing your report as if nothing happened.  If you are
not lucky, you might have to open the printer's manual and spend an hour
reading it.

(Entire job done in half a day - as long as you can live with the fear that
one day your quick-n-nasty will surface).

And if you have multiple page reports?
What the hell, if it works on page 1, then you might as well do it whenever
you find a "\f" or reach 66 lines.
(Add another half a day and practice denying it was you who did it).


Now you know why no-one ever asks me about printers :)

Jim Morcombe




<sbelt@dpcsystems.com> wrote in message
news:ljesb0tq4b75638ei20hp0u49vu0bkihra@4ax.com...
> Hello,
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: The most dangerous product the IBM mainframe has ever seen — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### The most dangerous product the IBM mainframe has ever seen

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T15:30:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20040603113028.16349.00000374@mb-m29.aol.com>`

```
In yesterday's Wall Street Journal (back page of the Marketplace section) there
was a story about Sun and Fujitsu teaming up to jointly develop future computer
systems, called the APL (Advanced Product Line).

The last paragraph quotes Scott McNealy (Sun's CEO):

"I think the APL will be ultimately the most dangerous product the IBM
mainframe has ever seen".

Well, competition is good. But what I found interesting is the implicit
recognition of the IBM mainframe as a major player even today. Most
competitors, at least publicly, denigrate the mainframe as not being a factor
in the industry any more. Now an open admission that mainframes are still a
significant part of the market. Somehow I find that encouraging.

Kind regards,


-Steve Comstock
800-993-9716
303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: The most dangerous product the IBM mainframe has ever seen

- **From:** "Mike" <NoSpam@StopSpam.org>
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T16:51:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I-udnXC2ONnyACLdRVn_vQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<20040603113028.16349.00000374@mb-m29.aol.com>`

```
> "I think the APL will be ultimately the most dangerous product the IBM
> mainframe has ever seen".

Oh what a bunch of crap. I have a monthly process that among other things
sorts 800 million to a billion data records depending on the time of year.
It usually completes in 2 to 3 hours on the mainframe. When Sun and Fujitsu
can match that kind of performance then they might be a dangerous product
for the mainframe. Otherwise, they're just blowing smoke. Most of the Sun
"boxes" I've ever seen run like a turtle.
```

#### ↳ Re: The most dangerous product the IBM mainframe has ever seen

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T23:31:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40bfb0b1.320103775@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<20040603113028.16349.00000374@mb-m29.aol.com>`

```
scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock) wrote:

> But what I found interesting is the implicit
>recognition of the IBM mainframe as a major player even today. Most
>competitors, at least publicly, denigrate the mainframe as not being a factor
>in the industry any more. Now an open admission that mainframes are still a
>significant part of the market. Somehow I find that encouraging.

Don't confuse high profit with high market share. 

IBM still derives 70% of its Net Profit from mainframes. The profit now comes
primarily from  consulting services, support and software rather than hardware.
IBM executives know that, but middle managers and sales people still think their
objective is Pushing Iron. Even contracting managers. In a talk with one of them
yesterday, she kept referring to contractors as "resources", meaning commodities
disposed of as easily as a used Kleenex. They have no concept of team building
or long-term strategy. They operate like a Body Shop, lurching from one
short-term project to the next. (I should talk.) Their acquisition of
Pricewaterhouse Coopers three years ago was intended by top management to change
that attitude. It hasn't filtered down to the trenches.

In a consumer economy where computer purchasing decisions are made by end-user
middle managers attuned to Brand Loyalty, the IBM name continues to have some
cachet. IBM is the Safe Choice .. for services as well as hardware. They'll hold
the manager's hand and promise to not let him or her look stupid in exchange for
a blank check.

Can such an exploitive relationship survive in an 'efficient' marketplace? The
short answer is yes. Human nature doesn't change. IBM's Watson tapped into it
just as his mentor, NCR's Patterson, had. At the micro level, the marketplace is
grossly INefficient. Sea changes, expressing real efficiency, come from top
management. Industries enjoying 90-100% Gross Profit impose no pressure for
change on top management. They are IBM itself, pharmaceuticals, financial
services and government. Also, companies with no pressure due to a monopoly,
such as utilities, some consumer products and vertical market domination.
```

## Thread: Homework — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Homework

- **From:** Julian <whateveryousend@iwontread.net>
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T17:35:14+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c9ng5b$dvk$1@news.brutele.be>`

```
Hi,

After many, many hours, I finally achieve my homework.
However I've some questions to determine is my program can be improved 
or corrected (both interest me).

I've 2 files. One is about the workers themselves (date of birth, 
contract number, etc..), the other is about their dayworks. Contract 
number is the "primary key" and "foreign key" I load the  first file 
into a table which is indexed. Then I read the second file, and I 
compute how many hours each worker had been working.

I'm using an indexed table because I cannot access element of my table 
  through its contract number (e.g. xxx478)

01 ouvrier occurs 20 indexed by idx.
            03 num-contrat          pic 9(6).
            03 nom                  pic x(30).
            03 datenaiss            pic 9(6).
            03 recolte              pic 9(4)  value zeroes.

idx is not declared in WSS.
20 is just the max size of the table, i use "bs" (pic 99 value 1) to 
know exactly how many workers I have.

when i read the second file, i've to locate precisly where the worker 
is in the table so i do the following :

	   set contrat-false to true.
            set idx to 1.
            search ouvrier
                when num-contrat(idx) = rec-num-contrat in rec-recolte
                    set contrat-true to true
            end-search.
            if contrat-trouve = 1 then
                add rec-poids to recolte(idx), total-recolte,
                total-recolte-jour
            end-if.

QUESTION : bs (real table size) is 9. Does the SEARCH look at the 20 
elements ? Or is there a way to say that the index should no go 
farther than the logical table size ?

77 contrat-trouve           pic 9.
            88 contrat-true                   value 1.
            88 contrat-false                  value 0.

if i both contract number are equal (the one found in the table by 
SEARCH and the other from the current record), i know that my worker 
exists.

further, i have to sort the table :
	sort ouvrier on descending key recolte.
so that i have the best worker first, and the worst last.

I just wanted to know if it seems good to you ? And i hope that my 
english is understable.

Thanks You !

BTW (for those interested)
http://homeusers.brutele.be/jmgg/iepscf/cobol/listings/exam.CBL
http://homeusers.brutele.be/jmgg/iepscf/cobol/labos/RESULTS
```

#### ↳ Re: Homework

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T15:58:57-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_6qdnZgT5pGNDCLdRVn-gg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<c9ng5b$dvk$1@news.brutele.be>`

```
Julian wrote:
> Hi,
>
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
> english is understable.

SEARCH checks each table entry until it finds a match - SEARCH stops on a
match.  SEARCH ALL hunts and pecks until it finds (or does not find) that
for which you are looking (SEARCH ALL requires the table to be sorted
first).

SEARCH ALL uses fewer (in some cases, FAR fewer) comparisons than SEARCH.
But until you get a table of several hundreds or thousands of entries,
you'll never see the difference.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Homework

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T14:33:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c9o5fl$k8n$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<c9ng5b$dvk$1@news.brutele.be> <_6qdnZgT5pGNDCLdRVn-gg@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:_6qdnZgT5pGNDCLdRVn-gg@giganews.com...

> SEARCH ALL uses fewer (in some cases, FAR fewer) comparisons than SEARCH.
> But until you get a table of several hundreds or thousands of entries,
> you'll never see the difference.

... umm ... no ... For most implementations, probably, but not a flat fact.

The standard provides enough restrictions on the table contents so that
doing, say, a binary search within those restrictions is arguably feasible,
but it does *not* require it.  All three standards ('74, '85, '02) use the
wording "a nonserial type of search operation may take place", and in
"standardese" the word "may" connotes permission to the implementor.  Had
the intent been that a nonserial search algorithm was *required* of the
implementor, the word "shall" would have appeared in this phrase.

I know of one implementation of SEARCH ALL that nonetheless does a serial
search, which *always* gives the same results as SEARCH for the same
selection criteria, whether the data is in monotonically-varying order by
the key or not.

In this instance, the implementation of SEARCH ALL once provided a binary
SEARCH, but enough users hated the behavior with out-of-order data that the
implementor had to defeat the feature.  A serial search does, after all,
meet the requirements of the standard.

(As to the user pressure on the implementor in this case, I'm reminded of
the complaints of a college roommate who hated his car:  every time he took
the engine more than 1,000 RPM over the tachometer's redline the fan belt
came off!)

A later implementation from this same implementor did a binary search for
SEARCH ALL (and received a few complaints); analogously, some auto
manufacturers have incorporated electronic engine-speed limiters in their
ignition systems.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Homework

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-06-04T16:09:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c9q6r6$pd9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<c9ng5b$dvk$1@news.brutele.be> <_6qdnZgT5pGNDCLdRVn-gg@giganews.com> <c9o5fl$k8n$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

On  3-Jun-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> I know of one implementation of SEARCH ALL that nonetheless does a serial
> search, which *always* gives the same results as SEARCH for the same
> selection criteria, whether the data is in monotonically-varying order by
> the key or not.

Does it stop searching when it gets past the key value?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Homework

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-04T11:46:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c9qg1p$26jc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<c9ng5b$dvk$1@news.brutele.be> <_6qdnZgT5pGNDCLdRVn-gg@giganews.com> <c9o5fl$k8n$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c9q6r6$pd9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:c9q6r6$pd9$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  3-Jun-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> > I know of one implementation of SEARCH ALL that nonetheless does a
serial
> > search, which *always* gives the same results as SEARCH for the same
> > selection criteria, whether the data is in monotonically-varying order
by
> > the key or not.
>
> Does it stop searching when it gets past the key value?

No, precisely because the issue is behavior of the WHEN condition regardless
of whether the table is in order or not; the next one in the table could be
*less* according to the key (contrary to the rules for SEARCH ALL).    And
if there are duplicates according to the key (also contrary to the rules for
SEARCH ALL),  this implementation always points to the first of those
duplicates.   This particular implementation provides defined and
predictable results for those cases that the standard describes the results
as undefined or unpredictable.  That, along with providing the results that
the standard defines as being neither undefined nor unpredictable given the
rules of the standard, is precisely what the users wanted -- *defined*
results with garbage data as well as with good data, instead of *undefined*
results with garbage data and good results *only* with good data.

For an unsuccessful SEARCH, the standard says the index value ends up
outside the range of permissible occurrence values; for an unsuccessful
SEARCH ALL, it says the index value is unpredictable ('74, '85) or undefined
('02);  in this instance the unpredictable/undefined value it ends up with
happens to be the same as for SEARCH.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Homework

- **From:** Julian <whateveryousend@iwontread.net>
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T23:33:59+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c9o55q$bkb$1@news.brutele.be>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<_6qdnZgT5pGNDCLdRVn-gg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<c9ng5b$dvk$1@news.brutele.be> <_6qdnZgT5pGNDCLdRVn-gg@giganews.com>`

```
so SEARCH does not care about the logical size of the table ? it will 
compares 20 times if no match is found ? even if there are only 9 
entries ?

is there any ways to tell SEARCH to not increment idx (my index) 
further than the logical table size ? (bs)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Homework

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T22:45:53+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1t6vb0dnegdv5uemeqmg49h33hdq9hnu1u@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<c9ng5b$dvk$1@news.brutele.be> <_6qdnZgT5pGNDCLdRVn-gg@giganews.com> <c9o55q$bkb$1@news.brutele.be>`

```
On Thu, 03 Jun 2004 23:33:59 +0200, Julian
<whateveryousend@iwontread.net> wrote:

>so SEARCH does not care about the logical size of the table ? it will 
>compares 20 times if no match is found ? even if there are only 9 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>further than the logical table size ? (bs)
Yes.

use the following.

last-tbl-item
01 tbl.
   05 tbl1 occurs 1 to 5000 times depending on last-tbl-item
 .......

and increment last-tbl-item for each item you add to the table.

The search will only search up to this value.




Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Homework

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T19:13:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10bvfje9eg21j39@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<c9o55q$bkb$1@news.brutele.be>`
- **References:** `<c9ng5b$dvk$1@news.brutele.be> <_6qdnZgT5pGNDCLdRVn-gg@giganews.com> <c9o55q$bkb$1@news.brutele.be>`

```
Julian wrote:
> so SEARCH does not care about the logical size of the table ? it will 
> compares 20 times if no match is found ? even if there are only 9 entries ?

yup...

> is there any ways to tell SEARCH to not increment idx (my index) further 
> than the logical table size ? (bs)

I've seen the folks that show you implementing Occurs Depending On (ODO) 
structure, and that would work.  What I do is make sure that the table 
is initialized to spaces before you begin, and then add the lines in all 
caps. (They don't have to be - I just did it that way to set it off from 
your code.  I'm also assuming that "nom" = "name".)

-=-=-=-=-

    set contrat-false to true.
    set idx to 1.
    search ouvrier
      when num-contrat(idx) = rec-num-contrat in rec-recolte
         set contrat-true to true
      WHEN NOM (IDX) = SPACES
         CONTINUE
    end-search.
    if contrat-trouve = 1 then
        add rec-poids to recolte(idx), total-recolte,
           total-recolte-jour
    end-if.

-=-=-=-=-

This will cause the Search to exit (as it's satisfied a When condition), 
and "idx" will be set at the first "hole" in your 20-occurrence table. 
(Of course, for ODO, you'd just add one to "bs" and you'd have the first 
"hole" as well.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Homework

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-04T07:09:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B9WdnbHz2-kd-13dRVn-uw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<c9ng5b$dvk$1@news.brutele.be> <_6qdnZgT5pGNDCLdRVn-gg@giganews.com> <c9o55q$bkb$1@news.brutele.be>`

```
Julian wrote:
> so SEARCH does not care about the logical size of the table ? it will
> compares 20 times if no match is found ? even if there are only 9
> entries ?

The table has 20 entries. Eleven of them are (say) blank, but to the
computer that's still an entry. What if you wanted to know whether there
were any blanks in the table?

>
> is there any ways to tell SEARCH to not increment idx (my index)
> further than the logical table size ? (bs)

Why would you want to do so, adding unnecessary code, that is?

Efficiency? Hardly. In a real-world application it is unlikely you'll be
asking for something that does not exist in the table. And even if you do,
the extra computation time is almost immeasurably small (a few seconds a
week in a heavy production environment).
```

#### ↳ Re: Homework

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T21:45:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40bf9494.312905852@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<c9ng5b$dvk$1@news.brutele.be>`

```
Julian <whateveryousend@iwontread.net> wrote:

>20 is just the max size of the table, i use "bs" (pic 99 value 1) to 
>know exactly how many workers I have.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>farther than the logical table size ?

Good question. The answer is yes, it looks at all 20 if the worker is not found.
There are two ways to stop this. 

.. 01 ouvrier occurs 1 to 20 depending on bs indexed by idx.

.. search ouvrier
           when idx > bs
               set contrat-false to true
           when num-contrat(idx) = rec-num-contrat in rec-recolte

>further, i have to sort the table :
>	sort ouvrier on descending key recolte.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>english is understable.

Yes to both. Your program looks excellent for a neophyte. Two minor stylistic
suggestions:

Change:
     perform aff-recolte until idx = bs
To:
    perform aff-recolte varying idx from 1 by 1 until idx > bs
    (delete initialization and set up by 1)

Change contrat-trouve to filler and change the test after the search to 'if
contrat-true'. Using both data name and 88 is bad form. If your compiler
permits, say 
    88 contrat-trouve                   value 1 false 0.

    when idx > bs
               set contrat-trouve to false


Robert
```

<a id="t-a825e666cf"></a>
## Thread: Reentrancy, Recursion, Threading, etc — 8 msgs

### Reentrancy, Recursion, Threading, etc

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-03T22:01:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jdNvc.20639$Tn6.13503@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
I just wanted to say that "re-entrancy" (sp?) was on the CANDIDATE list for the
2002 Standard at an EARLY stage.  During discussion, it was felt (accurately or
not) that the current (2002) and past (all versions) Standards neither "defined"
nor prohibited reentrancy.

So far, nothing that has been stated in C.L.C.  makes me think this is wrong.
Certainly the INTENT (and I believe the specifications) of "run-unit" and the
scope of what the Standard sets out to specify simply does NOT address multiple
run units executing simultaneously and how they "share" resources.  It *does*
specify what happens to BOTH working-storage and local-storage when multiple
instances of the same program are active within the same run unit.  (It also
addresses file section, etc - although that has not come up so far).

As at least one existing implementation supports reentrant code that is invalid
for recursion and recursive code that  is not reentrant; it is hard for me
(personally) to see how an "upwardly compatible" Standard specification for
these (that was interactive) could be introduced.

As far as "threading" goes, it is hard for me to imagine the STANDARD specifying
how multiple "thingies" (copies?) of a COBOL program within the same run unit
would work until/unless COBOL defines a way to "activate" multiple threads
within the same run unit.  I do KNOW that existing implementations deal with
this - and I think it would be "nice" if a future Standard better
defined/handled this.  It is certainly POSSIBLE (but not required) that this
could be addressed when/if "asynchronous" programming was introduced to the
Standard.
```

#### ↳ Re: Reentrancy, Recursion, Threading, etc

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-06T01:51:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40c25bda.127204998@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<jdNvc.20639$Tn6.13503@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>I just wanted to say that "re-entrancy" (sp?) was on the CANDIDATE list for the
>2002 Standard at an EARLY stage.  During discussion, it was felt (accurately or
>not) that the current (2002) and past (all versions) Standards neither
>"defined" nor prohibited reentrancy.

That's true.

>So far, nothing that has been stated in C.L.C.  makes me think this is wrong.
>Certainly the INTENT (and I believe the specifications) of "run-unit" and the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>addresses file section, etc - although that has not come up so far).

The Standard deals only with recursion, when it says an instance of
Local-Storage will be created upon entry and deleted upon exit. That's fine
because that's all recursive programs need. 

That's insufficient for multithreaded programs, which additionally require (at
minumum):
..language to start threads (exit program may be adequate to stop them)
..locks to prevent concurrent writes to shared memory (Working-Storage)
..language to sleep until an event occurs and language to signal an event

>As at least one existing implementation supports reentrant code that is invalid
>for recursion and recursive code that  is not reentrant; it is hard for me
>(personally) to see how an "upwardly compatible" Standard specification for
>these (that was interactive) could be introduced.

That's why it should be in the Standard -- because do-it-yourself extensions may
be flawed.

>As far as "threading" goes, it is hard for me to imagine the STANDARD
specifying
>how multiple "thingies" (copies?) of a COBOL program within the same run unit
>would work until/unless COBOL defines a way to "activate" multiple threads
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Standard.

"Asynchronous" is appropriate, although multithreading is the more common word. 

Multi-tasking was a 'first generation' attempt to do asynchronous computing with
then existing OS facilities. When they were found lacking, multithreading was
invented as a 'second generation' technology to provide better synchronization
services. As some CS type (Michael: are you listening?) will point out, better
mechanisms are already available, and will continue to emerge.

The challenge for a Language such as Cobol is to abstract asynchronicity so it
isn't tied to a particular OS implementation.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Reentrancy, Recursion, Threading, etc

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-07T11:06:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ca2aqv$1em7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<jdNvc.20639$Tn6.13503@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40c25bda.127204998@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert.deletethis@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:40c25bda.127204998@news.optonline.net...

> That's insufficient for multithreaded programs, which additionally require
(at
> minumum):
> ..language to start threads (exit program may be adequate to stop them)

PROCESS and CONTINUE in MCP COBOL ('68, '74 and '85), maybe?

> ..locks to prevent concurrent writes to shared memory (Working-Storage)

USAGE LOCK,  and the LOCK and UNLOCK statements, perhaps?

> ..language to sleep until an event occurs

USAGE EVENT, WAIT UNTIL event-name, and RESET event-name, maybe?

> and language to signal an event

> CAUSE event-name, maybe?

> That's why it should be in the Standard -- because do-it-yourself
extensions may
> be flawed.

I guess my position is that the *tools to allow* asynchronous processing
should be in the standard.  What should not be in the standard are extensive
specifications that limits the functionality for the sole purpose of keeping
the programmer from shooting himself in the foot.

> "Asynchronous" is appropriate, although multithreading is the more common
word.

May be, but the problem is that WG4 has blessed the idea of looking at
"asynchronous processing", but not the idea of "multithreading", and they
don't mean the same thing to all people.

> Multi-tasking was a 'first generation' attempt to do asynchronous
computing with
> then existing OS facilities. When they were found lacking, multithreading
was
> invented as a 'second generation' technology to provide better
synchronization
> services. As some CS type (Michael: are you listening?) will point out,
better
> mechanisms are already available, and will continue to emerge.

We've been doing multi-tasking, multi-threading, multi-programming and
multi-processing using both  reentrancy and recursion on Unisys MCP-based
systems with equanimity for around four decades now.  Examples of tools that
allow the *user* to do these things already exist.

My opinion is that if the user can declare a "lock item", and the user
transfers control to an asynchronous process that has visibility to that
item,  checks that lock and finds it still locked, the bug's in the calling
program for failing to unlock the lock when it was done with the item it
controlled, not with the standard or the compiler.    I believe such would
be the case for a number of the rules for asynchronous processing; it is up
to the user to ensure that his data *is* protected; all that is required of
the standard is that it provide standard mechanisms whereby the data *can
be* protected.

The less the standard requires be done "automatically" the better, I
believe.

 > The challenge for a Language such as Cobol is to abstract asynchronicity
so it
> isn't tied to a particular OS implementation.

I personally don't think it's that much of an imposition to declare three
new data types as Unisys MCP has them (LOCK, EVENT, TASK) and the syntax to
support access to them as described above.  The problem is that some would
regard "already exists in some OS implementation" (in this case, the Unisys
MCP architecture) as sufficient reason to reject the syntax *and* the
functionality on the basis that it is, prima facie, "tied to [that]
particular OS implementation" because the Unisys MCP operating environment
has been providing OS support for LOCKs, EVENTs and TASKs for quite a long
time, and other environments haven't.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reentrancy, Recursion, Threading, etc

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reentrancy, Recursion, Threading, etc

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-07T12:09:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ca2ei3$1h5l$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<jdNvc.20639$Tn6.13503@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40c25bda.127204998@news.optonline.net> <ca2aqv$1em7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <40C4B8F6.1020100@optonline.net>`

```

"Warren Simmons" <wsimmons5@optonline.net> wrote in message
news:40C4B8F6.1020100@optonline.net...

> Except, Chuck, when the USER had already extended or created software to
> accommodate the problems. I remember our use of IMS, TOTAL, and IDS
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> BACKED UP.

If the user has already extended or created software to accommodate the
problems, I would suggest that the standards folks would be very much
disinclined to *prevent* such programs from continuing to compile and run.

What I was trying to get at is that if the COBOL standard is to provide
support for asynchronicity, it seems to me that the basic functionality
requires user-declarable data items that describe events, locks and tasks,
and syntactic support for manipulating each.   I don't care what the USAGEs
are called, and I don't care what the statements or extensions are, either.

The Unisys MCP COBOL syntax I've described is independent of any other
software save the MCP itself, and I think it's a pretty good *functional*
model for *language syntax* supporting asynchronous processing with a
minimum of "behind the user's back" stuff.    If you wait for an EVENT that
no program ever CAUSEs, you might wait forever.  If you call a program that
requires that  a lock be reset before the program proceed, it's a good idea
to make sure you reset the lock before you call the program.  That sort of
stuff.  I don't care whether the lock is called a LOCK, or whether you CAUSE
or TRIGGER or PRECIPITATE or ACTIVATE an event, it's the concepts to be
supported by the syntax that are the concern of the standard.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reentrancy, Recursion, Threading, etc

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reentrancy, Recursion, Threading, etc

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-07T14:51:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ca2o04$1neu$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<jdNvc.20639$Tn6.13503@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40c25bda.127204998@news.optonline.net> <ca2aqv$1em7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <40C4B8F6.1020100@optonline.net> <ca2ei3$1h5l$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
And to amplify a little further on what I wrote in ...

> If you wait for an EVENT that
> no program ever CAUSEs, you might wait forever.  If you call a program
that
> requires that  a lock be reset before the program proceed, it's a good
idea
> to make sure you reset the lock before you call the program.  That sort of
> stuff.

If you wait for an "event" that nobody ever "causes", that's a program
error.  It's neither the system's job nor the compiler's job to make sure
that the "event" gets "caused", that's your job as application designer.

Likewise, if you call a program that requires a "lock" be "unlocked" before
some resource can be accessed, and the "lock" is still set, that too is a
program error, probably in the program that passes control to the one that's
expecting the available resource.

I grant that such application program errors can be difficult to catch or
even identify, but it's not the language compiler's business to try to catch
or identify them; it's the user's job.  It's the user's job to "cause"
events if programs dependent on those events are to wake up, and it's the
user's job to "unlock" resources when other programs cannot continue so long
as the resources are "locked", just as it is the user's job to make sure a
file is open before writing to it.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reentrancy, Recursion, Threading, etc

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-06-08T06:38:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40c55065.320908845@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<jdNvc.20639$Tn6.13503@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net> <40c25bda.127204998@news.optonline.net> <ca2aqv$1em7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert.deletethis@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:40c25bda.127204998@news.optonline.net...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>> CAUSE event-name, maybe?

MCP sounds very much like Unix threads, since copied by  MS and IBM.  As
discussed here, multithreading existed since the late '50s, but wasn't
standardized until the late '80s publication of a Unix library.  Sounds like it
may have been inspired by Unisys.The terminology is the same.

>I personally don't think it's that much of an imposition to declare three
>new data types as Unisys MCP has them (LOCK, EVENT, TASK) and the syntax to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>time, and other environments haven't.

Those objections are unlikely because all major platforms provide very similar
services.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Code Advise

- **From:** Leo Das <ld@invalid.nospam.nl>
- **Date:** 2004-06-06T15:27:25+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40C31BBD.9050705@invalid.nospam.nl>`
- **References:** `<sAjNa.48078$8B.44379@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <6dtvb0pmjsqk3pnih78n0jtuj9d720fusm@4ax.com>`

```
IF  total-credit-hours < 12
     STRING "*"        DELIMITED BY SIZE
            temp-name  DELIMITED BY SIZE
            INTO name
END-IF

As far as I know stringing is the only way to do it. You can leave out 
"delimited by size", because that is the default. I only added it 
because you indicated that you are a COBOL student.

I normally write everything in capitals, so the traditional way. The 
words in small letters are field names, so you can change them into the 
names you prefer.

Leo Das
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Code Advise

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40c3221c.177967089@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<sAjNa.48078$8B.44379@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <6dtvb0pmjsqk3pnih78n0jtuj9d720fusm@4ax.com>`

```
On Fri, 04 Jul 2003 18:21:12 GMT, "TerryMac"
><tmaccaro@rochester.rr.com> wrote:

>>I'm a Cobol student.  I have to write a program that reads a data file and
>>creates a report file.  One of my jobs is to make the program insert an
>>asterisk at the beginning of a name if total credit hours is less than 12.
>>
>>I'm at a loss.  Can someone help me out?

Want to get an A?

working-storage section.
01  name-with-asterisk.
     05  value '*' pic x.
     05  student-name pic x(30).
linkage section.
01  name-for-report   pic x(30).

if  total-credit-hours less than 12
    move name-field in input-file to student-name
    set address of name-for-report to address of name-with-asterisk
else
    set address of name-for-report to address of name-field in input-file
end-if
move name-for-report to name-field in report-line
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Code Advise

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-06-07T10:14:19-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ca1t7r$lka$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<sAjNa.48078$8B.44379@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <6dtvb0pmjsqk3pnih78n0jtuj9d720fusm@4ax.com> <40c3221c.177967089@news.optonline.net> <QbGdnQt5ubwL-VndRVn-iQ@giganews.com>`

```
In article <QbGdnQt5ubwL-VndRVn-iQ@giganews.com>,
JerryMouse <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:
>Robert Wagner wrote:
>> On Fri, 04 Jul 2003 18:21:12 GMT, "TerryMac"
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>mentioned somewhere.

Now, now... remember how some manuevers in the martial arts have names
like 'Two Cranes Approaching the Nest' or 'Enticing the Tiger Out Of Its
Lair'?  Mr Wagner might be indulging in a variant of 'Repulsing the
Brain-Leech'; this variant - 'Encouraging the Neophyte's Explanation' -
consists of supplying a student with code that works... but will cause the 
instructor to look up and say 'Where the *hell* did you get this from?!?'

For instance... how about something like:

    IF WS-TOTAL-CREDIT-HRS > 12
        MOVE '*' TO DET-LIN01-NAME(1:1)
        PERFORM D7124-MOVE-NAME-CHARS THRU D7124-MOVE-NAME-CHARS-EX
         WITH TEST BEFORE
          VARYING SUB1 FROM 2 BY 1
           UNTIL SUB1 > (LENGTH OF INPUT-NAME-FLD)
    ELSE
        PERFORM D7124-MOVE-NAME-CHARS THRU D7124-MOVE-NAME-CHARS-EX
         WITH TEST BEFORE
          VARYING SUB1 FROM 1 BY 1
           UNTIL SUB1 > (LENGTH OF INPUT-NAME-FLD).

...

D7124-MOVE-NAME-CHARS.
    
    IF DET-LIN01-NAME(1:1) = '*'
        SUBTRACT 1 FROM SUB1 GIVING SUB2
        MOVE INPUT-NAME-FLD(SUB2:1) TO DET-LIN01-NAME(SUB1:1)
    ELSE
        PERFORM D7126-MOVE-NAME-CHARS THRU D7126-MOVE-NAME-CHARS-EX.

D7124-MOVE-NAME-CHARS-EX.
    EXIT.

...

D7126-MOVE-NAME-CHARS.

    MOVE INPUT-NAME-FLD(SUB1:1) TO DET-LIN01-NAME(SUB1:1).

D7126-MOVE-NAME-CHARS-EX.
    EXIT.

... or something like that.

DD
```

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>>
>> Oh, I don't know. Isn't there a law of God, man, or the programming
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> *hell* did you get this from?!?'

I. I'm from Texas. Our fighting techniques range from "Kick Nuts" to "Blow
his ass away!" intersperced with "Worried him some" (the latter involves
barbed-wire and a thin metal rod).

II. More likely the instructor would say: "Just what is your major
malfunction, Fuckwit?"*

* Non-Texans would substitute "moron" for the last word.

---- Aside
We were discussing, on another newsgroup, Texas' quaint "paramour statue"
(now since repealed) that allowed a husband to kill his wife's lover without
penalty. During the research, I ran across this charming quote from the
bench:

Defendant (after sentence had been pronounced) "Why is it, your honor, that
I can kill my wife's lover and go free, but you're sending me to jail for
killing a horse!"

Judge: "Some men just need killing. There's no justification for killing a
horse."
```

```
Paul Raulersonv wrote:
> Now, *why* have I thought you were a Canadian all these years?
> Didn't know you were a fellow Texan. :) :)  (Okay, I am adopted
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> -Paul

I'm in Houston, the largest city in the nation with no zoning and whose
biggest cultural event is the Live Stock Show and Rodeo (total attendance in
ten days is larger than the annual attendance of the Astros, Texans, and
Rockets - combined).

We have almost no cultural events, no historical landmarks, no natural
wonders. Kinda like Manchester. It's hot, humid, and flat. We do have
bookstores and movies, though.

What we DO have is money, 70% of the nation's refining capacity, the largest
medical center in the world, NASA, and, importantly, LOW taxes.

I can't imagine how you could confuse me with a Canadian. My spelling,
perhaps?
```

```
> biggest cultural event is the Live Stock Show and Rodeo (total attendance
in
> ten days is larger than the annual attendance of the Astros, Texans, and
> Rockets - combined).

Which brings to mind the *San Antonio* Stock Show and Rodeo, once touted by
Dizzy Dean in a television commercial with the closing line "Don't fail to
miss it!".

All I could think of at the time was "Oh, no fear, I shan't!".

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Code Advise

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-08T08:07:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ca4ko8$30tv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<sAjNa.48078$8B.44379@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <6dtvb0pmjsqk3pnih78n0jtuj9d720fusm@4ax.com> <40c3221c.177967089@news.optonline.net> <QbGdnQt5ubwL-VndRVn-iQ@giganews.com> <ca1t7r$lka$1@panix5.panix.com> <M7WdnaV-7dkkSVndRVn-sA@giganews.com> <a66xc.23339$AU1.7687@nwrddc01.gnilink.net> <ssednfJImq-1rljdRVn-sw@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:ssednfJImq-1rljdRVn-sw@giganews.com...

> It's hot, humid, and flat.

I was told by friends in the diplomatic corps that emissaries stationed in
Houston from other countries received the same hazardous duty pay *for
climate* there that they received when they were stationed in Calcutta.  98
degrees Fahrenheit and 95% relative humidity for weeks at a time.  Hot and
humid does not begin to describe the summer climate in Houston.

The water table under William Marsh Rice University, which I attended for a
couple of years, was very near the surface.  I have heard from numerous
sources that the entire playing field of Rice Stadium is on a grating, with
an enormous pool beneath, which is constantly being drained by sump pumps.
The same is supposed to be true of any building on campus with a basement.
Whenever it rained the entire campus flooded; many is the time I slogged to
class in foot-deep water.  Another rumor is that the little rise on which
Hermann Hospital sits was formed by dredging William Rice's Marsh.  I
remember strolling along a walkway noticing the round holes in the grass
around trees full of (clear) water; in one of them there were several live
fish, which seemed to be thriving ...

And as to flatness:  in traveling home from Houston I always rejoiced when I
saw the first hillock around halfway to San Antonio (somewhere around
Schulenburg, as I recall).  I grew up in the Hill Country (first in Boerne,
then in Helotes) and spent eighteen years in San Antonio.   You have to go a
long way out of Houston to find anything that really qualifies as a "hill",
particularly along the IH10/US90/US90A corridor.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Code Advise

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-09T08:02:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1umdneuBXcPol1rdRVn_iw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<sAjNa.48078$8B.44379@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <6dtvb0pmjsqk3pnih78n0jtuj9d720fusm@4ax.com> <40c3221c.177967089@news.optonline.net> <QbGdnQt5ubwL-VndRVn-iQ@giganews.com> <ca1t7r$lka$1@panix5.panix.com> <M7WdnaV-7dkkSVndRVn-sA@giganews.com> <a66xc.23339$AU1.7687@nwrddc01.gnilink.net> <ssednfJImq-1rljdRVn-sw@giganews.com> <ca4ko8$30tv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
> news:ssednfJImq-1rljdRVn-sw@giganews.com...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> describe the summer climate in Houston.

We didn't build the world's first air-conditioned, domed stadium for drill.
We're at the same latitude as Cairo, but have the Gulf Stream to warm us.

>
> The water table under William Marsh Rice University, which I attended
> for a couple of years, was very near the surface.

It's better now. We got tired of that crap and lowered the water table
(upstream dam built by imported beavers, I think).

>
> And as to flatness:  in traveling home from Houston I always rejoiced
> when I saw the first hillock around halfway to San Antonio ...

We have a lot of hills, some as much as 150' high. We call them "freeway
overpasses."
```

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>>
>> I. I'm from Texas.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> other hand, is.'

I know Kinky Friedman. He writes a fair mystery novel and is a fair singer.
If you're looking for a good quote, consider: "I wasn't born here, but I got
here as fast as I could."

> (And if you think that was nasty... well, my original urge was to
> respond along the lines of 'Of course... now, which one of these
> techniques caused the Great Victory at the Alamo?')

The Alamo was but a battle. Santa Anna could not leave a fortified position
in his rear to threaten his supply lines. He therefore had to destroy the
fort. The time taken for that little aside provided Sam Houston sufficient
opportunity to win the war. As a result, Texas became an independent
republic and, when it later joined the United States, it did so by treaty
between two soverign nations - the only state to have done so.

>>>
>> Defendant (after sentence had been pronounced) "Why is it, your
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> accepted way of dealing with a broken-legged horse?

I'm just reporting what I ran into during my research. In general, though, I
think Texans are well aware of things horsey.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Code Advise

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-08T07:43:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WsCdnVH3y7_-KVjd4p2dnA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<sAjNa.48078$8B.44379@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <QbGdnQt5ubwL-VndRVn-iQ@giganews.com> <ca1t7r$lka$1@panix5.panix.com> <M7WdnaV-7dkkSVndRVn-sA@giganews.com> <ca2v25$h61$1@panix5.panix.com> <vipac0p213f8istchp9e3gf120pqgr2egd@4ax.com>`

```
berlutte@sympatico.ca wrote:
>
> Texas is becoming the object of deeper scrutiny. Law makers hiding,
> redistricting (wink). penal sytem under watch after the Iraqis war
> crimes, etc.

Yeah, we've had deserters (all Democrats). Redistricting, however, finally
prevailed. Texas will send from seven to nine new Republicans to the House
of Representatives in the fall. Considering that the current Republican
majority in the House is but eleven, a pick-up of seven is a HUGE increase.
This technique (redistricting) was far cheaper than winning seven contested
seats. All in all, a bargain.

I don't know what you mean about oversight of the penal system; we were
under a court order for a number of years (like twenty) but that expired
several years ago. Things are back to normal, now. Interestingly, Texas
inmates grow about 99% of their own food (except, perhaps, pepper), build
most of their own prisons, and manufacture, from scratch, their own clothing
and bedding. In so doing, they learn valuable skills that will serve them on
the outside - like mashing tomatos to make ketchup.

>
> Two class really. Enough money around to buy most men. A bit like
> Saudi Arabia and its extremist religion.
>
> Ghastly, really!

You, too, could have lots of money: Shut down the tax spigot. And there're
not two classes of folk here, just one - the well-to-do - with gradations.
We don't believe in taking from the poor to give to the rich - we believe
that wealth can be created.

>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> gain in March..."

Debt is like a firearm: it can be abused, but, in the main, is a Good
Thing(tm).
```

## Thread: Robert Wagner and 88 — 4 msgs

### Robert Wagner and 88

- **From:** "Jim Morcombe" <jim@byronics.com.au>
- **Date:** 2004-06-04T13:34:08+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c9p0sq$sci$1@yeppa.connect.com.au>`

```
While reading a bit of sage advice by Robert Wagner to a neophyte I came
across the following:

> Change contrat-trouve to filler and change the test after the search to
'if
> contrat-true'. Using both data name and 88 is bad form. If your compiler
> permits, say
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
 >              set contrat-trouve to false

What is this "false 0" tacked onto the end of the "88" line?

All my life I have lived with code such as:

01    IS-IT-AN-ALIEN                  PIC X.
        88  IT-IS-AN-ALIEN            VALUE 1.
        88  IT-IS-AN-EARTHLING   VALUE 0.

Am I now learning that I could do this instaed?

01    IS-IT-AN-ALIEN                  PIC X.
        88  IT-IS-AN-ALIEN            VALUE 1  FALSE 0.



Jim Morcombe
```

#### ↳ Re: Robert Wagner and 88

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-04T06:01:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LeUvc.21100$Tn6.10897@newsread1.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<c9p0sq$sci$1@yeppa.connect.com.au>`

```
WHEN SET TO FALSE (in an 88 level - and only the word "FALSE" is required) and
SET TO FALSE are part of the 2002 Standard - and are available as an extension
(already) in some compilers.  I know that Micro Focus supports it already, but
am not certain which other compilers already provide this support.
```

```
Jim Morcombe
```

## Thread: BREAKIN': ROYAL BANK O' CANADUH IN CHAOS - NEEDS CODIN' FOOLS ASAP — 40 msgs

### BREAKIN': ROYAL BANK O' CANADUH IN CHAOS - NEEDS CODIN' FOOLS ASAP

- **From:** berlutte@sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2004-06-04T11:52:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<emg1c016cqrl2v5n2660nrlk08hftt7alf@4ax.com>`

```

Is Yer Grandee ready to fly in if tawdry matter o' money are resolved?

http://www.theglobeandmail.com/servlet/ArticleNews/TPStory/LAC/20040604/ROYAL04/National/Idx

Five days of computer chaos leave bank's clients in limbo

By JOHN SAUNDERS AND RICHARD BLOOM
With a report from Erin Pooley
Friday, June 4, 2004 - Page A1 

In one of the worst messes since banks put their faith in computers,
today is the fifth day in which the Royal Bank of Canada cannot tell
its 10 million Canadian customers with any certainty how much money is
in their accounts.

Canada's biggest bank, suddenly a symbol of risks facing an automated
society, has a problem that has kept tens of millions of transactions
-- including what appears to be every direct payroll deposit it
handles -- from showing up in accounts for days at a time.

The chaos has hit other banks whose customers awaited transfers from
the Royal, notably in New Brunswick, where it processes payroll
deposits for more than 10,000 provincial employees. It is unclear how
many people have suffered hardship despite bankers' pledges to bend
the rules where possible to help those with pressing needs.

Vicky Deschï¿½nes, a New Brunswick Finance Department official, said she
could afford to wait a day or two for her pay, "but for some employees
this morning when I came to work, it was a concern for them and they
were asking: 'Am I going to pay fees for that? Am I going to be
penalized for that?' "

Bank officials said customers can still get at their accounts through
branches or bank machines and can withdraw money that was there before
the trouble hit, plus varying sums based on individual limits attached
to their client cards.

Judi Levita, a Royal Bank media-relations officer, said anyone facing
a crunch should make a personal plea. "You go into a branch, talk to
the branch manager, bring along your pay stub, because they can
usually pull [past transactions] up on a computer and they're going to
be able see, oh, yeah, John has this deposit made every other week,
etc., and we're sure it's sitting there."

In those circumstances, the customer could withdraw the money, she
said.

The bank also promised to reverse services fees and overdraft interest
charges incurred by its clients because of the glitch, including those
charged by other banks, and to consider paying "other reasonable
costs" on an individual basis.

Its nightmare began during what it called a routine programming
update. As best it could explain yesterday, its entire national system
failed to register withdrawals and deposits against customer balances
on Monday, on Tuesday and again on Wednesday, and by yesterday, the
system was stalled by the weight of the backlog.

Restoring data a day at a time, the bank hopes to get Wednesday's
transactions on-line today but warns that yesterday's transactions are
unlikely to show up until tomorrow.

"We have 155 people on the day shift working just on this alone, and
then we have another 90 people who are in doing nights and graveyard
shifts," said Ms. Levita, whose duties included trying to describe the
origins of the problem.

"I am not a technical person," she said. "It did have to do with
computer software and at this point I understand it did have something
to do with sequencing."

Sequencing of what?

"I honestly don't know. As I say, I mean, it's one of those tech
things."

None of the bank's technical people could discuss it, she said. "At
this point, no. They're too busy working on getting everything
corrected."

George Geczy, a software developer and computer consultant based in
Ancaster, Ont., guessed that the problem involves identification
numbers assigned to transactions.

"Obviously if somebody made a deposit before they made a withdrawal
then the bank needs to know the order that happened in. Programmers
rarely use time stamps any more because time can actually be a little
imprecise. Everything gets assigned a unique sequence number."

Such numbers can paralyze computers when gaps or duplications show up,
he said.

Perhaps the system "was expecting the next number to arrive -- the
next sequence number, the next transaction -- and didn't get the next
transaction and then it would go into a state where it was waiting for
that missing transaction. It wouldn't process anything else because if
it did move on past the missing number everything would be out of
order."

In New Brunswick, provincial pay deposits were expected to reach
employees' accounts by this morning at the latest. But in Ontario,
many provincial employees joined the list of those affected by delays
yesterday, Canadian Press reported.

One area of bank operations was not hobbled.

"I owe them a Visa payment, but this glitch hasn't affected their
ability to collect the money.
```

```
The hyperlink photo has 2 identical dolphins in it. It was used
in a case study on stress level at St. Mary's Hospital.

Look at both dolphins jumping out of the water. The dolphins are
identical. A closely monitored, scientific study of a group
revealed that in spite of the fact that the dolphins are
identical, a person under stress would find differences in the
two dolphins.

If there are many differences found between both dolphins, it
means that the person is experiencing a great amount of stress.

differences you may want to take a vacation.
```

```
brothers will keep us posted as this develops.
```

```
>
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> PL

I think that's unlikely.  I suspect that the problem was fixed within a few
hours of being detected.

However, by that point they had probably 'lost' a day's worth of processing.
For the bank the size of RBC, that means tens of millions of transactions
would have to be reprocessed.  And, while they're doing that, millions more
transactions would continue to come in, creating a huge backlog that
couldn't be cleared until the weekend.

Bad luck for them that the problem occurred so early in the week!  Worse
luck for RBC customers and payroll clients.
```

- **From:** Gus <Gus@nowhere.net>
- **Date:** 2004-06-06T06:54:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<2dzwc.6950$c76.4838444@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<CFswc.141439$tb4.5078598@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<emg1c016cqrl2v5n2660nrlk08hftt7alf@4ax.com> <40C252A1.BF71B6C0@mb.sympatico.ca> <CFswc.141439$tb4.5078598@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Don Leahy wrote:

> "Peter Lacey" <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
> news:40C252A1.BF71B6C0@mb.sympatico.ca...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> luck for RBC customers and payroll clients.

Don't know for certain about the "banking" side of things in the US, but 
if custodial accounts weren't posted & balanced with Tuesday's business, 
then you didn't open for business on Wed. until you were current. This 
was a major problem for us in the NYC area 11 Sept 2001 & the next few 
days. The SMAC (securities movement & control) applications HAD to 
settle trades Tue night so we could be open on Wed. While the US banks 
were on holiday until the following Monday, the non-US markets were open 
and trades entered before 11 Sept. had to be settled on time. It was 
very hairy for a couple of days with both the power & phone backups out 
along with the primaries - lots of sneakernet running tapes around NYC.
```

```
http://www.rbc.com/newsroom/

On the evening of **May 31st**, we experienced a processing disruption
during a programming update to one of our computer systems. As a
result, some client transactions (such as deposits, withdrawals, and
payments) for the business week ending June 4 were not properly
reflected in client account balances.
============================

Sounds like they had problems transiting for the 31 to the First o'
June.

Like someone played with the sliding ''windowin'' bar! 

No press release so far today as to the state of payroll.

But as our beloved Draft oft mentioned in his 'Life of Granny' serie: 

'Thou shall not relieth on press releases!'
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

```
This way please. Watch yer steps!

Roll it Gus!:

friggin' daily, was *again* dismissed by lunatic, impotent,

polly know-nuthin' failed third graders bone headed fags!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ PPI numbers postponed until...sometime im the future!

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** berlutte@sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2004-06-09T22:14:40-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<j6rfc0ti0lk2g0lkmma69ves3hnjpvlca3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<emg1c016cqrl2v5n2660nrlk08hftt7alf@4ax.com> <40C252A1.BF71B6C0@mb.sympatico.ca> <CFswc.141439$tb4.5078598@news20.bellglobal.com> <2dzwc.6950$c76.4838444@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <iROwc.38812$to.9479@twister.rdc-kc.rr.com> <8UVwc.2422$IQ2.2027834@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <0qq9c0peoo3at63b40j0602u9r279vjp9u@4ax.com>`

```
http://www.bls.gov/ppi/may_ppi_notice.htm
 
Delay of  Release of PPI for May 2004

The Producer Price Index (PPI) for May 2004, originally scheduled for
release on June 11, has been postponed until further notice.

BLS staff are working to resolve the unexpected difficulties in
calculating the index this month.

A revised release date has not yet been set but could be no earlier
than Tuesday, June 15. When a revised release date for the May 2004
PPI has been determined, it will be announced at least one day ahead
of time on this web page and through a news advisory.

The BLS expresses its apologies to those who experience any problems
as a result of this delay. 
========================================================= 

Sounds like after *they* can't fake the numbers anymore, *they* will
simply stop publishing the stats altogether. (except fer fellow
robbers, off course)

Merkans will have to use dead-reckoning, ear-to-the-ground and wet
finger tip fer direction.

How peculiar, reallly!
```

```
and/or c) the markets don't know jack about what's gonna happen.

Readers should consider going to cash and waiting this one out.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT everyone knows everyone. WAS: PPI numbers postponed until...sometime im the future!

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2004-06-11T17:30:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0406111630.69876833@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<emg1c016cqrl2v5n2660nrlk08hftt7alf@4ax.com> <iROwc.38812$to.9479@twister.rdc-kc.rr.com> <8UVwc.2422$IQ2.2027834@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <0qq9c0peoo3at63b40j0602u9r279vjp9u@4ax.com> <j6rfc0ti0lk2g0lkmma69ves3hnjpvlca3@4ax.com> <40c80f00.500803135@news.optonline.net> <10ch8klimc0ij05@corp.supernews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-75A1D8.21471010062004@corp.supernews.com> <40c96060.587184517@news.optonline.net> <wceyc.4628$0g3.822651@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`

```
Vince McGowan <SPAMvincemcg@nada.com> wrote in message news:<wceyc.4628$0g3.822651@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>...
> Robert Wagner wrote:
> 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> cousin Tony? Or, you were in the Army - do you know my cousing Tony?

As a New Zealander (population now just 4,000,000 and 30 years ago,
when I left these shores, around 3,000,000), I have become very used
to people all over the planet saying: "You're a Kiwi...D'you know
(substitute whatever names you like here)?"

Given our small population (we are focused on quality rather than
quantity...<G>) I guess it is a reasonable question. However, our long
skinny islands extend just on 1000 miles, so the chance of actually
living next door to any given person (or even knowing them in the same
community), cannot be expected to be high. Yet, on more than one
occasion, I actually DID know the people designated!

My standard response now is: "Probably. Us Kiwis are a tight knit
community and we believe in marrying our cousins, so I'm probably
related to the people you mentioned." This has the desired effect of
making them move on to the next party-goer, before they can find out
that I'm in the computer industry...(Once that gets out you've had
it...the rest of your evening will be spent listening to horrific
stories of how computers destroyed their lives, and requests for free
advice on how to stop Windows from hanging (as if THAT were possible
<G>)).

These days, if you meet me at a party I'm the Postman (everybody loves
the Postman...right?) from the non-existent country of Blowdonia
(people are reluctant to admit a piece missing in their knowledge of
Geography). During the evening, as I imbibe more alcohol, my
Blowdonian accent will vary from Scottish/Welsh/Irish towards
American/Australian/Kiwi until I finally collapse in the corner as an
incoherent heap (if it is a GOOD party...)

That way I get to end up in the kitchen with the real serious
drinkers...

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<emg1c016cqrl2v5n2660nrlk08hftt7alf@4ax.com> <40C252A1.BF71B6C0@mb.sympatico.ca> <CFswc.141439$tb4.5078598@news20.bellglobal.com> <2dzwc.6950$c76.4838444@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <iROwc.38812$to.9479@twister.rdc-kc.rr.com> <8UVwc.2422$IQ2.2027834@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <0qq9c0peoo3at63b40j0602u9r279vjp9u@4ax.com> <j6rfc0ti0lk2g0lkmma69ves3hnjpvlca3@4ax.com> <40c80f00.500803135@news.optonline.net> <10ch8klimc0ij05@corp.supernews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-75A1D8.21471010062004@corp.supernews.com> <40c96060.587184517@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
> 
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

We still get the odd person from England visiting Halifax or Toronto 
that wants to do a short side-trip to Vancouver. We point out that it 
would be closer to take a side trip to London, at least from Halifax. I 
think Toronto is about mid-point.

Foreign countries are always "further away".  I grew up 30 miles from 
Buffalo, but still considered it like the other side of the moon. 
Toronto, at 90 miles, was "local".

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: BREAKIN': ROYAL BANK O' CANADUH IN CHAOS - NEEDS CODIN' FOOLS ASAP

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-07T07:45:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<9r-dnfRBMrLN_lnd4p2dnA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<emg1c016cqrl2v5n2660nrlk08hftt7alf@4ax.com> <40C252A1.BF71B6C0@mb.sympatico.ca> <CFswc.141439$tb4.5078598@news20.bellglobal.com> <2dzwc.6950$c76.4838444@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <iROwc.38812$to.9479@twister.rdc-kc.rr.com>`

```
gripper5612 wrote:
>>
>> Don't know for certain about the "banking" side of things in the US,
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> one..

The true success story on 9/11 was the Wall Street Journal whose
headquarters was just down the street from WTC. They had a few minutes after
the attack before they had to abandon the building. During that half-hour
the IT people did a massive data dump to the WSJ backup facilities in New
Jersey. Meanwhile an executive ordered something like 1000 PCs and
associated network equipment (from Dell, I believe) for next-day deliver to
the backup site.

Other logistical operations were simultaneously underway, such as contacting
all editorial personnel to report to the alternate site.

Bottom line, the WSJ missed their publication deadline by thirty minutes for
the next day's edition. At 10:30pm on 9/11, the WSJ transmitted the 9/12
edition of the nation's largest circulation newspaper to their 19 printing
plants scattered around the world.

Somebody should write a book.
```

```
JerryMouse wrote:

> gripper5612 wrote:
> 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> headquarters was just down the street from WTC. 

Nothing special about the WSJ, we all did that then. My employer had 
real time backups at Comdisco (i.e., prod updates were mirrored remotely 
on Comdisco systems). My office was at 100 Church Street and the 
datacenter was on Barclay Street, 2 blocks north of 7 WTC. The WSJ 
didn't have a network of ATM's to get back online and AFAIK didn't face 
the kind of penalties banks face for missing various windows. I'm not 
demeaning what the WSJ did to stay in business, BTW, just pointing out 
their situation wasn't extraordinary.

Let me ask you, was the "true success story" after 911 publishing the 
WSJ or keeping the financial markets orderly?
```

<a id="t-06342b8441"></a>
## Thread: VMS to AS400 conversion — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`AS/400, iSeries, RPG`](topics.md#as400)_

### VMS to AS400 conversion

- **From:** kwfeese@charter.net (Ken)
- **Date:** 2004-06-05T12:02:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5613d666.0406051102.5a510b6e@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

We are in the process of a two year long project of converting our
data from running on OpenVMS Alpha machines to AS/400 DB2.  As I have
no hands on knowledge of DB2 nor any IBM requirements, I was wondering
if anyone had persued this type of conversion and what type of
pitfalls I should be conserned with.  I've been told that although
OpenVMS uses ASCII collating sequence and IBM uses EBCDIC, is this
correct?

Thanks in advance for your responses,

Ken
kwfeese@charter.net
```

#### ↳ Re: VMS to AS400 conversion

- **From:** "Paul Raulersonv" <pkrauleson@verizon.net>
- **Date:** 2004-06-06T03:14:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<p_vwc.10566$AU1.6867@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<5613d666.0406051102.5a510b6e@posting.google.com>`

```
Boy - are *you* going to have fun! <grin>
There are several considerations, the biggest one of which you have correctly identified as
being the ASCII/EDCDIC conversion. This may make some of your comparisons fail, or
more accurately, make a wrong selection.

More subtle issues you will encounter are also, since you are coming from a Vax/VMS platform,
not really going to present much of a problem. For example, the AS/400 does all it's arithmetic
in Packed Decimal format, just like a mainframe. It is *much* more efficient to make your
variables "packed" formats rather than binary. I do not *think* the VMS system supports packed
decimal, or if it does, I do not think many VMS programmers use it. I could be wrong there,
but I don't think you will have the issues of converting packed decimal information from
ASCII to EBCDIC. That *is* a real pain - I assure you of that!

Screens are going to be fun too, if you are using VT100/ANSI/etc. screens, you will need to translate them to AS/400 'green screen'
mode. The screen formats are stored separately on the
AS/400, and data is passed back and forth in some extremely sophisticated ways - but those same ways can be difficult to get an
understanding of, depending upon your experience base. If you, for example, understand BMS on a mainframe, it makes a lot of sense.
If you are used to say, UNIX termcap style entries, it is insanely difficult to make the conceptual jump.

File storage on an AS/400 is also different. First off, disks are called DASD - for historical reasons. Again, that is similar to
the mainframe, but it is not so clear to a VMS or UNIX person.
"Libraries" are roughly equivalent to directories, they can contain programs, files, and some other exotic objects. But 'files'
alone are pretty exotic - there are two basic kinds of files and a smattering of special purpose files out there.

Physical files are equivalent to a PDS (Partitioned Data Set) on a mainframe, and as far as I know, do not have an analog under VMS
or UNIX. (Nor under Windows to be honest.) The source code for your programs would be stored as 'members' of a file - essentially
you might think of them like 'blob' records in a database table, but they are not really that at all. :)  If you think of a physical
file as being a subdirectory of a library, but one that can only contain a *single format of file* - you come close to the idea. For
example, COBOL source programs are stored as 92 byte records in a physical file. (By convention, the file is called QCBLSRC or
QCBLLESRC, depending upon what compiler you use. This is convention only, not a standard or requirement.)
In the same library, you would probably have another physical file called QDDSRC, in which you would store the source code for
database and screen 'formats.'

To create a database file for example, the very basic structure is to write a DDS source file with a 'type' of PF (physical file),
and in the source you define the record format and keys. To actually create the database file, you use a command to 'compile' the
DDS source - the result being the real physical file in which your data will be stored.

If that sounds more like ISAM than Database to you, in a way it is. But the physical files are really database tables. :)  In fact,
DB/2 on the mainframe does something similar - at the core of DB/2, you will find a set of VSAM files! <grin>

All that is to say, it is a lot of fun to work on the AS/400, and I think you will grow to love the platform. In many ways, it is a
poor man's mainframe. I say that from the point of view of having a old P/390, RS/6000, and AS/400e here at the house, and an up to
date modern fully powered z800 Mainframe complete with Shark at work. Of all the machines, I admit to loving the mainframe world the
most, but the AS/400 is purely wonderful too.

Indeed, should you need consulting help with the AS/400, let me know. I'd be glad to help out a bit in my spare time - it really
sounds like you have a very fun project ahead of you!

-Paul
```

```
Paul Raulersonv a ï¿½crit :

> Boy - are *you* going to have fun! <grin>
> There are several considerations, the biggest one of which you have correctly identified as
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> decimal, or if it does, I do not think many VMS programmers use it.

VAX and Alpha (nothing to do with VMS!) support them, although the efficiency depends
much on the cpu board and firmware. But I agree this is not common, unless from programs
ported from mainframes.

> I could be wrong there,
> but I don't think you will have the issues of converting packed decimal information from
> ASCII to EBCDIC. That *is* a real pain - I assure you of that!
>

Best way is to "export" all data files in text form, convert from ASCII to EBCDIC and
then reimport them. If your files have no MRT, it should not be that difficult.

>
> Screens are going to be fun too, if you are using VT100/ANSI/etc. screens, you will need to translate them to AS/400 'green screen'
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I agree. I hope that like my shop, you have just one routine to do the terminal IO. If not,
best way is to find a converter or write one. BTW, is it straight COBOL displays, SMG$
routines or FMS ?

>
> File storage on an AS/400 is also different. First off, disks are called DASD - for historical reasons. Again, that is similar to
> the mainframe, but it is not so clear to a VMS or UNIX person.
> "Libraries" are roughly equivalent to directories, they can contain programs, files, and some other exotic objects.

Wrong. "Libraries" are "Libraries" on OpenVMS, either text or object libraries.

> But 'files'
> alone are pretty exotic - there are two basic kinds of files and a smattering of special purpose files out there.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This is really a diff: all source files are expected to be a member of a library,
while they are separate sequential files on OpenVMS (I don't know OS/400
(oops I can't remember the new name), but I worked twenty years ago
or 360/370's with MVS).

>
> To create a database file for example, the very basic structure is to write a DDS source file with a 'type' of PF (physical file),
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> > kwfeese@charter.net
```

```


Ken wrote:

>Hi,
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Ken,

May I ask why you are going from VMS to AS/400 given that the longevity 
of AS/400 is less known than that of VMS on Alpha and Itanium. If it is 
performance and price you're looking for, you won't find anything better 
than the new Alpha DS10, DS25 and ES40/45/47 range. You can get a plain 
DS10 for around ï¿½1000 (or less) or a fully kitted out ES45 with 
fibrechannel, 1/2 TB storage, 16GB memory, 4 processors for around 
ï¿½75,000 (that bugger boots up in less than 30 seconds !!!) and licensing 
is pretty reasonable too as they are all classed as desktop systems.

You may have guessed that I'm a VMS chap and have been for many years 
and I'm a little biassed.

Does your current system make use of things like CDD or maybe you have 
some embedded SQL in there talking to an Oracle database? If you could 
be a bit more specific on the components you use you might possibly get 
a better answer. Also, you now have the option to go onto an Itanium box 
(I've been playing with various languages on one recently). Keep your 
options open and don't forget that VMS is the ONLY operating system 
certified as secure by the US govenment (even hackers say 'don't bother'.

Ade
```

```
John
```

## Thread: Mainframe COBOL in Canada? — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Mainframe COBOL in Canada?

- **From:** "Mike" <mike_hughesdon@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2004-06-06T16:47:16+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vUHwc.205$Ki4.6@newsfe2-gui.server.ntli.net>`

```
Hi,

I'm thinking of a move to Canada but I don't know much about the market
there for jobs, could someone give me some pointers?  I'm a mainframe
developer, nowadays working in COBOL with IMS and DB/2 in the finance
sector.  The sort of things I'd be interested in are;

1.  Where are the COBOL users based, Toronto, Vancouver etc, big cities or
in the provinces too?
2.  Is there a typical version of COBOL/toolset used?
3.  How are jobs advertised, through agencies, web sites, newspaper ads?
4.  What's the job market like currently?
5.  Is there a healthy contract market out there, or is it mostly permanent
positions that are available?
6.  What's the pay rate for contract and permanent?

Thanks for your help.

Mike
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe COBOL in Canada?

- **From:** Doug Scott <dwscott@ieee.org>
- **Date:** 2004-06-06T22:35:05+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VA.00000684.003b0196@ieee.org>`
- **References:** `<vUHwc.205$Ki4.6@newsfe2-gui.server.ntli.net>`

```
Mike,

> Thanks for your help.

I can't give you any assistance on any of those questions, I'm afraid, 
but I can give you some general warm feelings. I know three people who 
have moved there (I've lost contact with them, otherwise I'd give you 
details). Each one hasn't looked back, and when asked wouldn't come 
back to the UK.

---

Doug

dwscott@ieee.org
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe COBOL in Canada?

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-06-06T17:57:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40C3A15D.4B69FFE7@mb.sympatico.ca>`
